- 1 MINUTE AGO: New Reports Detail Harry's $25 Million Challenge — Massive Financial Pressure Surfaces: Kind: captions Language: en A $25 million figure now frames the immediate financial and legal landscape for Prince Harry. This sum is central to a London courtroom proceeding that could either validate his strategic decisions of the past 6 years or result in a financial liability with significant long-term implications. Crucially, this specific legal action represents just one component of several converging pressures now impacting the Duke of Sussex in early 2026. To grasp how an individual born in to immense inherited wealth and institutional protection could now confront a potential financial destabilization, it becomes necessary to review the initial choices that led to this trajectory beyond the public narrative. When Prince Harry and Meghan, the Duchess of Sussex, withdrew from their senior royal duties in January 2020, they undertook a course of action almost unparalleled in modern British royal history. Their stated intention was essentially, "We will generate our own income. We will not rely on the institution. We will construct a new enterprise." This was a bold move. Indeed, it was largely unprecedented. And in its early stages, it appeared to be a viable path. Within months of their departure, the couple secured what was then described as a landmark content agreement with Netflix. The reported valuation stood at $100 million, a nine-figure arrangement with one of the most influential streaming platforms globally. To external observers, this seemed to confirm that the Duke and Duchess had successfully leveraged their royal standing into a commodity the entertainment sector would remunerate generously. Subsequently, Spotify entered the picture with another significant deal involving another prominent brand. That particular contract was estimated to be worth between $20 million and $25 million contingent on the specific reporting sources. Between these two agreements alone, the Sussexes had on paper accumulated over $100 million in potential earnings from their creative endeavors. Then, a gradual shift began, initially subtle, then rapidly accelerating. The Spotify agreement was terminated in 2023 after the couple delivered merely 12 episodes of Meghan's Archetypes podcast. That was the entirety of the output against a contract valued in the tens of millions. This dissolution was notably public. Bill Simmons, Spotify's head of talk content, asserted on his own podcast that the couple were, in his direct terms, "fucking grifters." This was not an anonymous disgruntled source circulating rumors to a tabloid. This was a senior executive at a major global audio platform publicly expressing frustration over a multi-million dollar investment that had yielded minimal discernible value. This Spotify incident resonated throughout Hollywood. It reached executive boardrooms. It influenced how studio leadership perceived the Sussex brand, thereby setting the precedent for subsequent developments. On the Netflix front, the couple's documentary Harry and Meghan, released in late 2022, served as their flagship endeavor. It achieved its objectives, generating substantial viewership figures, considerable controversy, and widespread discussion. This was precisely the kind of content Netflix had anticipated when establishing the partnership. However, reports indicated that Netflix believed it had extracted maximum value from the couple. And when the original agreement concluded in 2025, it was not renewed in its initial form. The $100 million Netflix deal recently expired in 2025, as Desert News reported. Harry's polo-focused documentary subsequently attempted to compensate for this reduction. It did not. The polo documentary reportedly garnered only around 500,000 views in early 2025 and was ranked 3,436th among all Netflix productions for the first half of that year. For an individual whose name once commanded global attention, this ranking constituted not merely a disappointment, but a commercially devastating outcome, as stated by International Business Times. Meanwhile, Meghan launched her lifestyle brand As Ever, accompanied by the polished and meticulously curated promotional efforts the couple typically executes effectively. Yet, market data presented a less favorable reality for As Ever. The platform received just under 197,000 monthly visits, a figure that significantly trails the 812,000 visits registered by Gwyneth Paltrow's Goop. Even considering a 20% site-wide discount during Thanksgiving in the highly competitive premium lifestyle sector, this disparity does not represent a minor setback. It signifies a structural challenge, according to International Business Times. Therefore, this was Prince Harry's financial posture entering 2026. His primary revenue channel, Netflix, had either ceased or been substantially curtailed. His Spotify collaboration had imploded publicly and painfully. His memoir, Spare, published in January 2023, achieved massive sales, but the associated income is not perpetual. Book advances and royalties are front-loaded. They do not indefinitely sustain a high-cost lifestyle. And it is indeed a high-cost lifestyle. The couple's Montecito residence, acquired in 2020 for $40 million, is understood to have been financed through multiple mortgages, with reports indicating a $5 million down payment and approximately $9 in outstanding mortgage debt. A source familiar with the couple's financial situation commented directly, "They should have bought a $7 million house free and clear. They did not," as reported by Reality Tea. Beyond the mortgage, there is the security expenditure. Their personal security detail alone is reported to cost approximately $3 million annually, year after year. There are no breaks, no budget reductions, because Harry's security threat level is not a factor he fully controls. It is a direct consequence of his identity and public profile. When a former senior British royal resides in Southern California, the security requirements do not adjust to accommodate budgetary constraints. As noted by Reality Tea, consolidating the mortgage payments, the security detail, and the operational expenses associated with public appearances requiring specific wardrobes and brand development necessitating staff, the financial calculations begin to demonstrate considerable pressure. Sources indicate they expend between $2 million and $3 million annually on security, in addition to monthly payments on a reported $10 million mortgage, and the supplementary costs inherent in their overall California lifestyle, as reported by Yahoo! Now, layer upon all these factors the developments affecting their charitable foundation. When Harry and Meghan established the Archewell Foundation in 2020, they articulated its mission as active engagement and positive impact. It garnered extraordinary initial support. In 2021, donations reached $13 million. This marked its peak. It also proved to be the commencement of a steady and protracted decline, according to International Business Times. Archewell's expenditures have surpassed its revenue in recent years, prompting substantial concerns regarding its long-term viability. The most recent tax filings, made public in December 2025, elucidated the full scope. In 2024, donations decreased to just $210,000, while expenses climbed to $510,000, resulting in a deficit of $250,000. The charity was disbursing more than double its incoming funds. Reserves diminished to $8.5 million, as cited by Black Enterprise Plus Two. The structural response involved a rebranding. The Archewell Foundation transitioned into Archewell philanthropies, adopting a new operational model. Instead of directly executing programs, the organization would utilize a fiscal sponsorship arrangement where another nonprofit entity manages legal and financial administration while Harry and Meghan guide charitable priorities from a leadership position. The couple's spokesperson characterized this as an evolution. Critics described it as a retreat. What transpired subsequently conveyed the situation more clearly than any press release could. This restructuring led to what observers depicted as a near total collapse of staffing. James Holt, who had directed Archewell since 2021 and collaborated closely with Harry for almost a decade, departed and relocated to the UK. The chief communications officer resigned. The director of programs and operations, a role with a reported salary of $146,000, was released. Three additional staff members were reportedly terminated just before Christmas 2025. And according to various reports, some of these employees were informed their positions were ending because the charity was closing. While the couple's representatives challenged this specific phrasing, the charitable entity in its prior form was effectively dissolved. In total, more than 20 employees are said to have left the Sussex Archewell organization since its inception. Royal expert Hillary Ford, a closely followed commentator on Sussex matters, characterized this pattern as further evidence of the dysfunction and instability within their household. But here is the aspect of this narrative that elevates it from a discussion of unsuccessful entertainment deals and a struggling charitable organization into something considerably more consequential, the legal action. To fully comprehend the current financial, legal, and reputational stakes for Prince Harry, it is essential to understand his presence in London in January 2026. It is also crucial to recognize why, once in that courtroom, his testimony under cross-examination became one of the most intensely observed and discussed legal proceedings involving a royal in over a century. Prince Harry has been engaged in a protracted conflict with the British tabloid press. This is not a metaphorical conflict, but a literal legal battle conducted in the High Court of England involving actual legal counsel, documented evidence, and genuine financial exposure for both parties. He has framed this campaign as a mission deeply personal and rooted in his conviction that the press contributed to the demise of his mother, Princess Diana, and has subsequently targeted him and his family for decades without accountability. He achieved a partial victory in this campaign in 2023 when he prevailed in litigation against Mirror Group Newspapers. In January 2025, Harry reportedly secured an estimated $12 million in damages from the publisher of The Sun, Britain's most widely read tabloid, in a settlement described as a significant inflection point in the phone hacking litigation landscape. The publisher issued what it termed a full and unequivocal apology. For Harry, this admission of wrongdoing was, by his own account, more significant than the monetary award. However, then came the decisive confrontation, one his own father, King Charles, allegedly once referred to as a suicide mission. In January 2026, Harry appeared at London's High Court to face Associated Newspapers, the publisher of the Daily Mail and the Mail on Sunday, in what commenced as a nine-week trial. He was not alone. The claimant group accompanying him included Sir Elton John and his husband, David Furnish, actress Elizabeth Hurley, and Baroness Doreen Lawrence, whose symbolic presence in any British courtroom carries substantial weight given her decades of advocacy against institutional injustice, as reported by CNN. David Sherborne, the barrister representing the claimants, initiated proceedings by stating his intention to demonstrate clear, systematic, and sustained use of unlawful newsgathering at both the Daily Mail and the Mail on Sunday. The allegations span three decades encompassing voicemail interception, landline tapping, and a variety of surveillance methods that Sherborne argued were so deeply embedded in the paper's culture that editors and executives could not have been unaware of them, according to Lawyer Monthly. Associated Newspapers has consistently denied any wrongdoing. Its legal representatives have characterized the allegations as firmly denied. Unlike News of the World and ultimately The Sun, the Daily Mail has not previously been engulfed by the phone hacking scandal. This trial marks the first occasion his son has undergone a full judicial examination of these allegations, and both sides understand the stakes are exceptionally high. The estimated combined legal cost for both parties had already reached the equivalent of $53 of $53 5 million. This figure merits contemplation. A $53 million legal engagement Associated Newspapers alone was anticipated to spend the equivalent of $25 million on this case, while the claimants, including Prince Harry, were projected to incur $18 4 million, as reported by CNN Newsweek. And here is the legal reality that has financial analysts, royal commentators, and insiders closely associated with the Sussexes genuinely concerned. If Harry is unsuccessful, if the court rules in favor of Associated Newspapers, he would almost certainly be mandated to cover a significant portion of the defendant's legal costs. If allocated proportionally among the claimants, Harry's individual share of the defendant's costs could reach approximately $6 8 million dollars. When added to his own legal expenditures, the total financial exposure resulting from a loss in this trial alone could vastly exceed anything he has previously spent on litigation, according to Newsweek. While that legal exposure is substantial, the emotional developments occurring within that courtroom are, by any measure, equally significant. On January 22nd, 2026, Harry provided evidence under cross-examination. He became visibly distressed towards the conclusion of a compelling session that lasted nearly two hours, informing the court that Meghan's life had been one of absolute misery due to media intrusion. This was not a meticulously prepared media appearance. This was an individual under oath subjected to challenges and pressures that no press conference or Netflix documentary had ever demanded. The cross-examination was described as grueling. Observers in both the public and the press were divided. Some perceived a man finally articulating the case he had endeavored to make for years, while others interpreted the more complex scenario, as stated by Lawyer Monthly. Because here lies the fundamental tension of this entire situation. Prince Harry has cultivated an identity centered on the premise that the British press functions as an antagonist. His memoir, his Netflix documentary, his public pronouncements over several years all have been constructed upon this narrative. The Daily Mail trial offers his opportunity to substantiate this claim in a court of law. However, if he fails to provide such proof, he not only risks losing the case and potentially millions of dollars, but he also undermines the very premise of his public stance. Meanwhile, back in California, domestic pressures are also intensifying. Insiders are now reporting that Prince Harry and Meghan are experiencing genuine friction behind closed doors concerning financial matters. Sources describe the Duchess as feeling deeply defensive when her commercial aspirations are questioned, even by Harry himself. One account specifically stated that Meghan becomes extremely defensive when accused of being overly focused on revenue, and that Harry's questioning of her approach is cutting deeply. The same source remarked that he was born into immense wealth and privilege, never needing to concern himself with the broader financial picture. Now he must, and this shift, according to individuals close to the couple, is generating considerable friction, as reported by the news. The couple's recent visit to Australia elicited accusations that charitable activities were being conflated with commercial opportunities after outfits Meghan wore during engagements with sick children and tragedy victims was subsequently promoted on the shopping platform with which she has affiliations. The optics were unfavorable. The controversy was pronounced and it introduced another layer to the increasing public skepticism regarding the actual purpose of the Sussex operation and who primarily benefits from it, as reported by the news. A well-placed source predicted a five-year window before their lifestyle undergoes significant alteration. That assessment was made several months prior. It is already beginning to appear accurate, according to Reality Tea. So, what is the comprehensive financial and operational status in May 2026? The Netflix agreement that funded the initial years of their American residency has ceased or been substantially reduced. The Spotify partnership dissolved in public embarrassment. The Archewell Foundation has been largely restructured and reestablished as a considerably leaner operation with a fraction of its original personnel. Meghan's lifestyle brand is struggling to establish competitiveness in a saturated market. A nine-week trial in London is exposing Harry to both legal and financial risks unlike any he has previously encountered. His security expenditures are continuous. His mortgage obligations are significant, and the inheritance he received from his mother Diana, his grandmother Queen Elizabeth, and his great-grandmother funds, intended to support him throughout his life, are being steadily depleted by the costs of the lifestyle he chose to cultivate, as noted by Reality Tea. King Charles has reportedly declined to meet with Harry during recent US visits. Royal experts indicate that both the King and Prince William feel they can no longer place trust in Harry, particularly given the manner in which the couple has publicly criticized the royal family in recent years. That avenue, the one that might once have provided Harry a financial bridge back to institutional support, even informally, appears to be closed, as cited by IMDb. What renders this narrative compelling is not solely its celebrity dimension, though that is undeniably a component. It is the underlying structural reality. Harry made a deliberate calculation when he departed the royal family. He wagered that the global audience would continue to pay for his narrative, purchase his brand, and consume his content indefinitely. The world did remunerate him once. It paid substantially for one documentary, one memoir, and one series of interviews. However, the mechanism that facilitates such significant payouts requires continuous reinvention, and continuous reinvention demands either exceptional creative output or unwavering commercial discipline. The evidence of the past three years suggest the Sussexes have struggled to consistently deliver either. The $25 million figure currently under discussion, the headline number that initiated this analysis, is not a singular sum. It is a composite. It encompasses the $25 million that Associated Newspapers is allocating to contest Harry in court. It represents the potential legal exposure he faces if he loses. It includes the unrealized value of the Spotify deal. It accounts for the annual shortfall experienced by his charity. It embodies the security expenditure that recurs every year irrespective of available funds. It signifies the cost of a life designed during a significantly different financial context than the one Prince Harry currently navigates. None of this suggests Prince Harry and Meghan are facing imminent bankruptcy. They are not. They retain assets, reserves, and a level of name recognition that most individuals globally will never possess. However, the fundamental financial calculation has shifted. They're not presently accumulating wealth. They are defending it, and there is a profound distinction between those two positions. When a prince testifies under oath in a courtroom regarding his wife's suffering, while simultaneously outside that court, his charity is shedding staff, his streaming agreements have concluded, his lifestyle brand is underperforming, and his security costs amount to millions annually, that transcends mere royal gossip. That is an account of what occurs when an extraordinary exit strategy encounters ordinary economic realities. Harry sought freedom, and freedom, as it now appears, does not include a fixed allowance. The Daily Mail trial still has several weeks to run. The verdict, once rendered, will resonate far beyond the confines of the High Court. Should Harry prevail, he secures a legacy-defining legal victory and potentially compels a reassessment within one of Britain's most influential media institutions. If he is unsuccessful, the financial blow will be substantial. The reputational impact may prove more severe, and his narrative, the one he has spent years endeavoring to control, will take a turn that no ghostwriter, no Netflix documentary, and no press release will be able to alter. In either scenario, the coming months will provide definitive insight into whether the decision Harry made in January 2020 was an act of resolve or the most significant miscalculation of his life. And at this juncture, with accumulating expenses, an active legal timeline, and narrowing income streams, the numerical indicators are not in his favor.

- What happens to your brain without any social contact? - Terry Kupers: Kind: captions Language: en Everyone needs time to themselves, and peaceful solitude has stress-relieving benefits. But being alone takes on an entirely different dimension when it creeps up or is forced upon you. When that's the case, the effects can be surprisingly extensive. And though different people experience distinct effects at different times, symptoms tend to become more severe and persistent the longer one's isolated. When someone is involuntarily confined to one space indefinitely— for days, weeks, months, or even years— alone and without productive tasks, their body will likely undergo numerous changes. Let's take a look at what may happen and why. Early on, stress hormones may spike, and as time passes, that stress can become chronic. Social interactions and meaningful activities are essential for emotional stability. This may be because they provide us with what researchers call “social reality testing”— a sort of sounding board where we can gauge how rational our perceptions are. So, when someone’s deprived of those kinds of communication and tasks, their sense of identity and reality becomes threatened. Their thoughts spiral and their impulses take the reins, setting the stage for depression, obsessions, suicidal ideation, and, for some, delusions and hallucinations. Over time, this prolonged agitation can cause the brain's limbic system, which regulates fear and stress, to become especially responsive and hyperactive. Meanwhile, the prefrontal cortex, the brain’s hub for reasoning and moral judgment, may shrink, impairing one's focus, memory, and cognition. Overall, the balance shifts from rational thinking towards emotionality. And as someone remains in this state, the imbalance becomes ingrained, making them more prone to bouts of anxiety, rage, and irrational actions. Isolation will also affect other parts of the person's health. They may lose sense of time and have difficulties sleeping. They're more likely to experience heart palpitations, headaches, dizziness, and hypersensitivity. And they may also lose weight because of stress-induced digestive issues and poor appetite. One can attempt to cope by establishing the healthiest routine possible under the extreme circumstances, including robust exercise, reading, and writing. But that can only do so much. The United Nations, many human rights organizations, and experts classify this kind of forced, prolonged isolation as torture. And yet, it’s something imprisoned people in many countries endure. Also called “solitary confinement” or “restrictive housing,” the practice is most common in the United States. In 2019, more than 120,000 US prisoners lived in solitary confinement, spending 22 to 24 hours a day in mostly windowless cells spanning roughly six by nine feet. Quaker groups introduced solitary confinement to US prisons in the late 1700s, as an alternative to corporal punishment, believing it could bring about reflection and penitence— hence “penitentiary.” But the practice quickly faced criticism, from public figures all the way to the Supreme Court. Charles Dickens, for one, condemned solitary confinement as “worse than any torture of the body.” Its use dwindled, but then, in the 1980s, alongside more punitive, tough-on-crime laws, US prison populations skyrocketed. As prisons grew crowded, incidences of protests, rebellions, and violence grew, and prison authorities increasingly used solitary confinement to maintain control. Many people have been placed in solitary confinement for minor, nonviolent infractions, like talking back to prison guards. And solitary confinement is harmful to everyone, but many who’ve experienced it have pre-existing mental health disorders, which it only exacerbates. Solitary confinement also seems to have lasting effects that make readjusting to life outside of a cell difficult. People who have gone through solitary confinement are three times more likely to show signs of post-traumatic stress disorder. And they commonly report experiencing shifts in their personalities, increased anxiety and paranoia in otherwise ordinary situations, and difficulty concentrating and connecting with others. Some states have restricted the use of solitary confinement in cases involving serious mental illness, children, or pregnancy, and some have adopted 15 or 20 day limits for everyone. But laws that regulate solitary confinement aren’t always enforced— and prison authorities have created loopholes. Yet solitary confinement does immense damage that is contrary to rehabilitation, while failing to reduce prison violence. Meanwhile, other countries have centered more humane approaches. Norway, for example, imprisons far fewer people per capita than the US while spending five times more per prisoner on accommodations, classes, and work-release programs. Norway also sees far fewer people return to prison after release, with one of the world’s lowest rates of recidivism, indicating we tend to get better together.

- Why do crowds move us? - Deeply Human, BBC World Service: Kind: captions Language: en Being part of a psychological crowd can amplify feelings that we already have It can amplify the experiences of events. Crowd psychology explore how individual behaviour is impacted… when large crowds group together. In crowd psychology, we distinguish between physical crowds and psychological crowds. Physical crowds are people who just happen to be in the same place at the same time, but there's no real kind of social connection. No feeling of being together as a group between them. On the other hand, you have psychological crowds. Now, psychological crowds are our main area of interest in crowd psychology, because these are crowds where people feel like they're part of the same group with the others in the crowd and there's a feeling of unity connectedness between them. You feel like they are closer to you. They're more like you. You have more positive feelings towards them, you have more cooperative behaviour. And you also have feelings of empowerment, of feeling like together, you can achieve change, because you're there together as a collective with the same goals, the same interests. Psychological crowds can bring people from different backgrounds together. Researchers have studied Hajj to understand how. You get pilgrims from all over the world, who are going there for a common purpose, different nationalities, different languages, even different religious denominations. And they're going there for the same purpose. And you see amazing examples at Hajj of people who are supporting each other who are sharing resources, on the basis of the shared group membership. You see this feeling of being part of the group makes people feel safe, even in really high densities, partially because they expect that others will keep them safe for being in that same group. Researchers say when you’re in a crowd you feel you belong… you feel safer, more empowered and more likely to help others. We're more influenced by people in our group, we tend to trust them more, we rely on them more, we think that they're more likely to help us and others.

- How Childhood Emotional Abuse Affects You In Adult: Kind: captions Language: en ever wonder why the punchline to every psychology joke always has something to do with either our childhoods or our issues with our parents or why people lying on a therapist couch tend to answer the question so tell me about your problem with it all began when I was little it's because on some level we all intuitively understand that our childhood experiences play a big role in shaping who we are and what we believe about others ourselves and the world around us that's why so much of psychology research theories and Psychotherapy focuses on understanding the impact Early Childhood experiences have on a person one particular line of research in the field focuses on adverse child experiences and the therapy built on these studies known as psychodynamic approaches aims to ameliorate the detrimental impact it can have on us even while into adulthood the US Children's Bureau defines adverse childhood experiences or Aces as traumatic events that occur during childhood such as abuse neglect illness injury bullying or pure victimization the long-term effects of such experiences can manifest either physically psychologically or behaviorally surveys show that children who were victims of emotional abuse often grow up to have overall poor health receive lower education are more likely to suffer from poverty weight gain obesity and significant psychological stress but what about their story Beyond these numbers what are other important but often over looked ways the abuse still lingers and hurts them in the present let's dive even deeper into how a childhood emotional abuse affects you even in adulthood number one it makes you anxiously attached ever heard about attachment Theory pioneered by John bulby and Mary answorth attachment Theory posits that there are generally two kinds of attachment Styles we develop as a result of our early experiences with our parents or caregivers that is secure attachment and insecure or anxious attachment anxious attachment is then categorized as either preoccupied when you have a positive view of others but a negative view of yourself leading to self-doubts insecurity and cleanness dismissive when you have a positive view of yourself but a negative view of others which makes you distant and overly self-reliant or fearful disorganized the attachment style of those who have experienced abuse people with a fearful disorganized attachment style tend to be very wary and guarded around others as well as being extremely sens to rejection conflict and abandonment and as adults they often struggle to form lasting positive relationships as a result of their unresolved attachment related trauma which brings us to our next Point number two it stunts your emotional development being emotionally abused as a child stunts your emotional development because it teaches you all the wrong things about life instead of learning important skills like emotion regulation self-awareness and relationship management victims of emotional abuse often grow up to develop issues such as emotional codependency self-blame and social isolation number three it leads to self-destructive patterns when someone comes from a home that fails to provide them with a sense of Safety and Security important to every child's development more likely than not that child will grow up to develop self-destructive patterns and maladaptive coping mechanisms instead according to a meta survey done by the mental health center of destination hope these can manifest in a number of ways such as in their behaviors developing addictions delinquencies Suicidal Tendencies or their thought patterns low self-esteem depression anxiety number four it compromises your quality of life taking all of the things we've talked about into account it should come as no surprise then that many researchers have found that the experience of childhood emotional abuse is associated with poorer quality of life thankfully many Studies have also highlighted that a lot of victims of abuse can and do recover thriving later in life despite their adverse childhood experiences the trouble is most of them unfortunately have to struggle to get there number five it puts you at higher risk of mental health problems last but certainly not least childhood emotional abuse can continue to haunt us even years afterwards by putting us at higher risk of developing certain mental health issues such as depression and anxiety all forms of child abuse have also been found to be strongly correlated with PTSD substance abuse and suicide if you are struggling with your experiences of childhood emotional abuse know that help is available the best thing you can do for yourself is to reach out to a mental healthc care professional today and begin healing from the pain of your past consider this the first day of your journey towards mental Wellness Recovery and self-discovery what's one way you will no longer let your childhood emotional abuse affect you

Uh I mean, in lie of having a big sort of massive spreadsheet model of the economy and how all these things interact and what the waitings are, I'm kind of, you know, got spreadsheet in my head that's kind of a bit of a gut feel. I mean, yeah, there's there's a few things going on in there. I mean, you know, the second round inflation effects, people might start to hear that mentioned. It's all about it's not just that straight up um immediate, hey, price at the petrol station went up. That's that's sort of direct first round impacts that that's what the Reserve Bank can't control. They're not necessarily worried. I mean, they're going to be worried about it, but um only to the extent that that flows through to lasting so-called second round effects, which is where a trucking company to, you know, to get goods around the country that they're paying more for fuel as well. So, they're going to have to raise their selling prices. That feeds into input costs for somebody else. You know, a supermarket has to charge more because it's costing them more to get the goods in. um farmers, you know, diesel prices going up for for that production. I mean, farmers are largely price takers. I guess they can't really set prices, but still across the economy, that second round impact is the the wider, you know, that flow through from from higher fuel prices, what that does to costs of production and then how that feeds through to kind of more lasting inflation. Um the manufacturers, the they use energy as well, so their costs are going up. They're going to have to pass that on if they can. I mean in a in a sluggish economy there's there's there's a limit to how much price rises can be passed through but you know all these things get really circular and there's kind of this interaction thing. So, so yeah, that the second round impacts are all it's it's that it's that that flow through to wider economy, wider production costs, how that comes through to inflation. And I guess also how it impacts inflation expectations, you know, if people are thinking, gee, this thing's going to last for a while. Petrol prices are going to be higher for a while, diesel costs are going to be higher for a while. You know, I'm going to have to actually pass this through. I can't absorb it. I'm going to have to pass it on. So, you know, the inflation kind of becomes a bit more embedded and and and that's where uh the Reserve Bank will get more concerned about it if it's it's passing through to what people are are expecting as well because that plays a role in in what the Reserve Bank does. So, um but yeah, at the same time, if things are costing more, if you if you're paying more for your petrol, if if firms are paying more for petrol, diesel, all of this, you know, there's less discretionary money available for other things. I mean, households are are going to have to pay more at the petrol station. there's less to spend at the grocery shop or or wherever at the restaurant. So, you know, the uh overall spending takes a takes a hit from that. So, there's, you know, there is circularity here and this is what will go into the big treasury models and the reserve being models. I've just got one in my head which I'm kind of sort of weighing all this up, but um yeah, so it's it's definitely not clearcut. Um, but I I think for me, you know, there's the big uncertainty about how long it'll last, but I guess I just probably worry more about the wider economic impact. It's it's the it's the real economy. If um if people having to pay more for for petrol, if this is um you know, crimping business spending, business activity, then eventually there's going to be a a downwards economic impact here and especially global uncertainty as well. I mean, you know, we just talked about the GDP numbers. it was pretty much held up by agriculture. If if we can't export as much, if you know, if shipping's disrupted, um all of that stuff, that that's going to that's going to hurt the economy for sure. And and with the spare capacity that already exists, uh you know, that will hopefully hold inflation a bit lower than it otherwise might have been, but then that weakening economy would would bring down inflation as well. So um you know I tend to lean more towards the side of of of um sort of being cautious about the economy and that should sort of keep some sort of level of reassurance at the Reserve Bank in terms of how much they really need to react with higher interest rates. But uh they've only got one target and that is the inflation rate. And so you know almost whatever the economy is doing they've got the target of inflation and if inflation's going up it gets uh gets a we bit awkward. So, I'm I'm conscious of that sort of trade-off as well and that um conflict there between weighing up the balance of higher inflation versus the weak economy. So, yeah, that's what other people have to weigh up, I guess, when they make these decisions, but for me, in the back of my mind, I just think this this this can't be good for the economy. Yes, there's inflation impact in the near term, maybe the medium term, but ultimately this is a negative story for the economy. So, that should eventually bring inflation back down. And you know, the thing I guess that's been maybe missing if you want to put it that way in the housing market has probably been a a confidence factor, a sort of job security factor. Maybe that's starting to come back. But then boom, along comes this this Iran thing. And that confidence factor is surely going to to go missing again. So we're maybe not there yet, but um yeah, there's a a lot to watch. >> Yeah, there is. And that's why I think it's so intriguing at the moment, right?   6. @newscomauhq [1 likes]: Grim picture for Australia’s first home buyers

#breakingnews #australia #housingmarket #rentalproperty 

The great Australian dream — owning a home — is slipping further out of reach, especially for young people.

With house prices continuing to smash records, new projections paint a confronting picture of what’s ahead if current trends continue.

Property platform Coposit analysed data from the ABS, CoreLogic and Your Mortgage to forecast median house prices through to 2050. Using a long-term average growth rate of four per cent, the outlook isn’t encouraging.

Sydney’s median house price, currently around $1.55 million, could soar to $3.88 million by 2050. In Melbourne, the median could climb from $953,000 to $2.56 million. Perth’s median house price — $895,000 this year — is projected to reach $2.4 million, while Brisbane’s could jump from $1.06 million to $2.84 million.

Coposit CEO Chris Ferris says the numbers show how quickly affordability is slipping away, especially in major cities where wages simply aren’t keeping up. He warns that Australia is “creating a generation of renters” — young people who no longer believe home ownership is within reach.

According to the ABS, the average age of first home buyers is now 37. And many are leaving capital cities altogether. In mid-2025, Sydney alone accounted for 60 per cent of the net outflow from all capitals to regional areas. Most of those movers headed to regional New South Wales or Queensland.

But not everyone is sympathetic. Property investor Jack Henderson made headlines earlier this year after saying that “only rich people should expect to own a home in Sydney.” He compared it to New York, arguing that big global cities will always be expensive and not everyone can afford to live there.

Still, for many Australians, the idea that home ownership might no longer be part of the national dream is a sobering thought — one that raises questions about what kind of future young people can realistically hope for.

- New Jersey Housing CRISIS: Inventory COLLAPSE Triggers BUBBLE WARNING: Kind: captions Language: en New Jersey home prices jumped nearly half in 5 years, yet the lines to buy keep getting longer. Here's the picture. In October 2025, the median listing price across New Jersey sat at $550,000. That is 44.7% higher than October 2019, when the median was $380,000. Prices even crested at a state record of $590,000 in July 2024 before easing back. But if you expected that to cool the rush, think again. In October, there were just 17,600 homes for sale statewide, down from roughly 40,400 in October 2019. That is a gap of 56.4% the third biggest shortfall versus pre-pandemic levels in the country. The result feels like a paradox. Inventory has climbed on an annual basis for 19 straight months, and prices have posted five consecutive months of annual declines. Yet supply remains so tight that buyers still have to sprint. Look closely at who's sprinting. New Jersey's draw stretches far beyond its borders. In the third quarter of 2025, New Yorkers hunting outside the five boroughs sent 12.9% of their out-of-city listing views across the Hudson. That share marked the biggest annual increase among the top 10 destinations for those city dwellers, up 1.4 percentage points from the same months in 2024. The trend lines point toward staying near the orbit of New York City. In that same window, 14.1% of New Yorkers' out-of-city views stayed within New York State itself, up 1.1 points year-over-year. By contrast, Florida captured 10.4%, [music] but its draw from New York City slipped 1.3 points from a year earlier. Pennsylvania landed 10.7%, up 1.2. Connecticut and North Carolina each took 5.1% followed by Ohio at 5%, Massachusetts at 3.5, Michigan at 3.2, and Georgia at 3.2. What is pushing this surge into New Jersey? Start with access, jobs, and the lifestyle trade that keeps Manhattan close but not [music] mandatory. Several of New Jersey's counties within the New York City metro area rocketed up 44 positions to rank as the 158th hottest large market in September. In the same spirit, two Garden State zip codes, Wayne and Martleton, ranked among the hottest in America for 2025. Listings there drew 3.6 times more online views and sold 30 to 42 days faster than the national average. Add to that a deep pool of buyers who skew older and wealthier than the typical American household with stronger credit profiles and bigger down payments. It is a competitive cohort that can move quickly and decisively, but demand without supply has consequences. New Jersey faces a shortage of more than 200,000 homes for low-income residents. More than 1/3 of households in the state are cost-burdened, spending over 30% of their income on housing. And the new homes trickling onto the market are often out of reach for the middle. The premium for new construction, what a buyer pays for a brand new house relative to an existing one, stands at 74.1%. That spread is among the steepest in the country and a blunt signal that most new builds are landing in the high end of the market. Mix in baby boomers holding tighter to the houses they have, and the pipeline narrows even further. Then there's Montclair, the elegant Essex County town that serves as a microcosm of this moment. Education is a magnet there, literally. The public school district's magnet system, paired with high ratings, has long been one of the town's greatest draws. Even a looming $19.6 million school deficit hasn't dented buyer appetite. Yet in September 2025, the median listing price in Montclair was $1.2 million. Homes Homes that month sold at an average of 16% above their asking prices. October saw more than 30 homes close. And by mid-November, nine more had sold. One address tells the story. 14 Upper Mountain Avenue hit the market at $849,900. 800 It didn't linger. The seller accepted an offer $150,000 above that list. That is the market flexing in real time, tight inventory, confident bids, and a buyer willing to pay a premium for a specific place [music] in a specific town. Today, Montclair sits firmly in what agents call a seller's market. More people want in than there are homes to buy. But Montclair also underscores the fine line between allure and risk. On December 9th, residents will vote on whether to raise taxes to address that $19.6 million deficit. Signals suggest a hike could flow through to rents, pressing affordability in a town already prized for its schools and proximity to New York. If the magnet system were to weaken, if jobs continue to be cut, if a state monitor downplays the program, some fear the district's reputation would erode. If that happens, the snowball could start. Diminished school prestige begets softer demand, which begets market recalibration. None of that is preordained. For now, demand remains unwavering. But the vote marks a hinge moment. >> [music] >> Zoom out to Jersey City, and the gravity intensifies. Between 2020 and 2024, the city's population climbed 7.5% apartment rents there rose 2.4% over the past year, roughly double the national pace. The average rent across all bedrooms and property types >> sits around $2,500. It is not a budget market. Jersey City's cost of living runs 26% above the national average, with housing expenses 74% higher and utilities 3% higher. And yet, it continues to pull. In a widely watched ranking of the top places to watch in 2026, Jersey City landed at number two. Northern New Jersey ranked seventh. Brooklyn took fourth. Manhattan placed ninth. The Northeast's secret weapon right now is proximity and pulse. Low apartment vacancy and the ongoing pull of office mandates have brightened the appeal of living one subway stop or one PATH ride away. Under the surface, policy is redrawing the map. In March 2024, New Jersey enacted one of the nation's toughest enforcements of the Mount Laurel doctrine, the constitutional rule that every municipality must provide its fair share of affordable housing. The statute requires towns to update master plans and zoning to allow more affordable homes for low-and-moderate-income families. It streamlines approvals to make it easier to build what is required. It adds teeth, too. Municipalities that miss deadlines or obstruct affordable housing risk losing their legal protection from exclusionary zoning lawsuits. The signal is unmistakable. The state wants more homes in more places [music] for more kinds of families. The new governor-elect has pledged to push that through. On day one, she promises to declare a state of emergency on utility costs and freeze utility rates for both homeowners and renters. The next steps target the core bottlenecks. Enforce the 2024 law, increase new home construction that meets the needs of all residents, expand first-time homebuyer assistance, and stop diversions from the affordable housing trust fund so dollars earmarked for affordability reach projects that can move. Her campaign put it bluntly, "Housing costs are up over 50% in just 5 years." The freshest statewide measurement pegs the 5-year increase at 44.7% through October 2025. Whichever statistic you prefer, the slope is steep. The slope is also shaped by New York City. In October, the average rent there was $4,462. Rent stabilization protects millions, and proposals to freeze rent for more than 2 million tenants have surfaced again. If that freeze takes hold, some market veterans warn free market rents could climb higher because fewer rentals would rotate back onto the open market. The city's new leadership has floated headline ideas. Free buses, city-owned grocery stores, a corporate tax raised to match New Jersey's 11.5% rate, and a flat 2% tax on the wealthiest 1% of residents. Those changes would require separate approvals, and there is no clarity yet on what will pass. But capital seeks certainty. In the days after the election, some investors put deals on hold, including two multifamily building sales, waiting for the fog to thin. Buyers do not always wait. A mortgage originator in Queens described clients looking to get out of New York as quickly as possible, including one who was willing to swallow a prepayment penalty just to move sooner. Agents beyond the five boroughs, from New Jersey to Connecticut, have reported a surge of inquiries from high-income New Yorkers eyeing a relocation in the wake of the vote. That early pattern sits atop the third quarter's numbers, where again, 12.9% of New York City's out-of-town listing views targeted New Jersey. The tilt toward nearby markets is unmistakable. >> Even Florida, long the sun-soaked favorite, saw its share of New York City views slip by 1.3 points year-over-year to 10.4%. >> Pennsylvania has momentum, too. 10.7% of New York City views in the quarter, and a case [music] bolstered by pricing that can be dramatically lower than New York City. Philadelphia's median list price was $384,900 in September, nearly $375,000 less than the city's median that same month. In Pittsburgh, the typical home was $254,950 in $250,000 October. Back in New Jersey, the contrasts are sharp. There is genuine progress. Supply has posted year-over-year gains for 19 straight months and prices have fallen on an annual basis for 5 months in a row. But those improvements live inside a bigger squeeze. Compared with October 2019, the state still had 22,800 fewer homes for sale in October 2025. That is 56.4% lower supply despite rising demand. The buyer pool is older, wealthier, and credit strong and remains active in specific corridors. In places like Wayne in Passaic County and Marlton in Burlington County, listings attract 3.6 times the average number of eyeballs and go under contract as much as 6 weeks sooner than typical USA >> listings. That's one side of the market. The other side often stares at a wall. A shortage north of 200,000 affordable homes leaves low-income households with limited options. More than one in three households statewide is spending above 30% of income on housing. New construction tilts luxury with a 74.1% premium versus existing homes. That premium is not simply a rounding error. It is the line between who gets to buy new and who must bid on [music] the existing stock and at what cost. Jersey City tests those boundaries. A 7.5% population bump in just 4 years does not happen by accident. Transit access, skyline views, and a slate of new towers have tightened vacancy. Rents rose 2.4% over the past year. The average across all units sits around $2,500. The city's placement as the number two market to watch in 2026 codifies something visible on the ground. Cranes [music] in the air, sandwich shops packed at lunch, lights on late. Just down the list, Northern New Jersey at number seven hints at a broader canvas from Hoboken to Newark to Montclair to the bedroom suburbs that push into Morris and Somerset counties. Still, even in the bright spots, friction accumulates. Jersey City's cost of living runs 26% above the national average. Housing expenses sit 74% higher. Utility costs are 3% higher. For a young couple stretching to make rent or a first-time buyer trying to land a starter home, those extra percentages are not abstract. They are the difference between getting in and getting squeezed out. >> New Jersey's pipeline must adapt. More homes that teachers, nurses, mechanics, and mid-level tech workers can afford near trains and jobs. That is where the March 2024 law matters. It tells every town, "Make room. Update your master plan. Fix your zoning. Create the pathways for developers to build homes that meet your fair share. Miss the deadlines and the courts can open you to lawsuits. Meet them and you gain the legal cover and the blueprint to add supply where it is needed." The next governor has said she will enforce those rules, expand first-time buyer assistance, and freeze utility rates on day one to lighten a monthly bill that keeps climbing. Local decisions will color how that plays out. Montclair's December 9th vote is one example. A yes on taxes might stabilize school funding and preserve the magnet system that underpins the town's appeal, but it could also raise rents and monthly costs. A no could deepen cuts and test the district's reputation. Realtors across town are watching closely. In the near term, activity remains brisk. More than 30 homes sold in October and nine had sold by mid-November. In September, buyers were still paying, on average, 16% over ask. Market heat can endure a lot until it can't. Across the state, the heat still shows up most vividly near transit. Both of the gubernatorial candidates in the recent race talked about focusing new homes around mass transit. That is not just rhetoric. In the hottest New Jersey zip codes, listings sell 30 to 42 days faster than the national average. And in the broader New York City metro area, New Jersey counties jumped 44 spots to number 158 in September in terms of market hotness, a composite of how fast homes sell and how many shoppers are looking. Even with statewide supply 56.4% below October 2019, these pockets can pull in new residents without asking them to give up their commutes or their weekend rhythms. Watch the financial edges. New York City's average rent of $4,462 in October concentrates minds. If a rent freeze stabilizes more than 2 million apartments, the spillovers might change. Free market rents could inch higher, especially if fewer rent-stabilized units reset into the market. Employers pushing return to office expand the radius of desirability around Midtown and Lower Manhattan. That is one reason for Northeast markets. Jersey City, Brooklyn, Northern New Jersey, and Manhattan cracked the top 10 places to watch in 2026. Low vacancy meets proximity. Proximity meets demand. On the capital side, the new mayor's ideas, free buses, city-owned grocery stores, a corporate tax lifted to New Jersey's 11.5% level, and a 2% levy on the top 1% need approvals at other levels of government. But uncertainty alone can tighten purse strings. On day one after the election, two sizable multifamily sales were put on pause as buyers and lenders waited for clarity. That wait can shift attention. Some investors will drift from the five boroughs to Hudson County or across to Bergen and Essex. Some will sit tight and do nothing. Some will go where the math still clicks. Dallas, Miami, Houston where the top 10 placement reflects different blends of growth and cost. Jersey City's number two slot and Northern New Jersey's number seven signal that many will stay right here and build. For buyers in New Jersey, the math begins with scarcity. homes for sale in October 2025 is a modest step up from the troughs of the last few years. But it remains a gulf from the 40,400 that turned up in October 2019. That 56.4% shortfall won't close overnight even as inventory posts 19 straight months of annual gains and even as prices slip year-over-year for the fifth month running. Bidding wars can still break out where schools, transit, and tree-lined blocks align. For sellers, the window is nuanced. In Montclair, a listing at $849,900 can still jump $150,000 above ask. In Wayne and Marlton, the traffic comes in heavy. 3.6 times typical views and the offers often arrive sooner than expected. In Jersey City, rents rose 2.4% over the last year and an average $2,500 monthly ask keeps many renters pinned in place, limiting move-up flow but cushioning landlords' expectations. Elsewhere, an older, wealthier, credit strong buyer set tilts demand toward the mid and upper tiers where that 74.1% new construction premium lives. Sellers who can price into that zone without overshooting will find an audience. For renters, the squeeze is genetic. More than one in three households statewide are already cost-burdened. In Jersey City, the cost of living is 26% higher than the national average and housing expenses are 74% higher. Across the river, New York City renters paid an average of $4,462 in October. Policy levers, the proposed rent freeze in New York City, the day-one utility rate freeze next door, are aimed at steadying the ride. Their timing and bite remain open questions. For builders and town officials, the stakes are legal and local. The Mount Laurel framework is no longer a suggestion. Master plans and zoning must be updated to make room for affordable homes. The process is streamlined to get projects moving. Miss the deadlines and you risk losing the legal protection that shields against exclusionary zoning suits. Hit them and you might finally widen a pipeline that is now skewed toward the 74.1% new home premium, the luxury tier that leaves most middle-income families on the outside looking in. So, what comes next? Base case, New Jersey stays tight but navigable. Expect the modest slackening to continue, more inventory on an annual basis after 19 months of gains, and some ongoing price softening after 5 months of annual declines. But do not expect a flood. Compared with October 2019, available listings remain 56.4% lower and that gap will not close by magic. Demand from nearby markets should stay strong. In the third quarter, 12.9% of New York City's out-of-city views were already aimed at New Jersey and post-election inquiries in the region have ticked up. Micro markets with schools and trains, Montclair, Hoboken, Jersey City, Maplewood, Summit, will continue to act like their own planets with gravity tuned to the block. Two watch items could shift the path into 2026. First, implementation of the March 2024 affordable housing statute. If towns update their master plans and zoning on time, and if approvals speed up as designed, the mix of what gets built could change more homes for low and moderate-income families, more projects near mass transit, and a gradual dent in that shortage of more than 200,000 affordable units. Failures to comply could invite lawsuits and slow walk the very additions the market needs. Second, New York [music] City's policy arc under its new leadership. If rent freezes and tax changes advance, lenders and landlords may reassess values. Some capital could stay paused, and a tranche of buyers and businesses may hunt harder in New Jersey. If rhetoric softens or proposals stall, the pause may lift and flows could normalize. In either case, the cross-river tie is tight. What happens there shows up here. New Jersey's housing story has always been about proximity and possibility. In 2025, it is also about scarcity and scale. One town weighs a December 9th tax vote to stabilize a beloved magnet system while listings in that same town sell at 16% above ask. One city adds 7.5% to its population in 4 years while charging an average of $2,500 in rent and still ranks as the number two market to watch. One state posts 19 months of rising inventory and 5 months of annual price declines while sitting listing count. And one set of policies, zoning updates, utility freezes, first-time buyer pathways, could open doors that have been locked too long. In the end, the math is the map. 44.7% price gains in 5 years statewide. 17,600 homes for sale in October versus 40,400 6 years earlier. A 74.1% premium for new construction. More than 200,000 affordable homes missing. 12.9% of New York City's would-be movers eyeing New Jersey. On one block in Montclair, an $849,900 listing pulled in a $150,000 over ask offer. On one city ledger in Jersey City, population up 7.5% since 2020 and rents up 2.4% in the past year. These are not abstractions. They are choices that accumulate into markets. If you found this breakdown useful, share it with someone who's weighing a move, and subscribe to stay grounded in the numbers shaping where we live next.

- When Someone You Love Dies,There Is No Such Thing as Moving On | Kelley Lynn | TEDxAdelphiUniversity: Kind: captions Language: en Transcriber: Asmaa Sheikh Warak Reviewer: Eunice Tan Best-selling author and widower C. S. Lewis said in the opening line of his brilliant book "A Grief Observed," "Nobody ever told me that grief felt so much like fear." It's a powerful statement: fear. But fear of what? The fear of losing yourself. The fear of growing old alone. The fear that this intense pain will never stop. The fear of forgetting the sound of his voice or his laugh or that others will forget him, that his life won't have mattered. Grief makes you feel isolated, alone, terrified, and damaged, and scared of absolutely everything. On October 27th, 2006, at the age of 35, I married my very best friend, Don Shepherd. Four years and nine months later, my healthy, active, beautiful husband left for work one morning and never came home. They found him collapsed on the floor - a massive heart attack. No symptoms, no warnings, no goodbyes - just here one minute and then, boom, gone. In the past five years or so since my husband's death, I've become friends with and met a lot of other widowed people. A few months ago, a dear widower friend of mine gave me a challenge. He said, "Kelly, I want you to change the world." "Is that all?" I said to him. "I will get on that right after my morning cup of coffee." But when I stopped to think about that concept, a favorite phrase of mine came to mind: "Change your mind, and change the world." In other words, the way that people see or perceive an idea has to change in order for everything surrounding that idea to also change. So I'm going to speak the truth today about grief and then wait for that truth to then become contagious. Any widowed person or any person who has lost someone they love to death will tell you about the well-intentioned but sometimes insensitive comments coming from those on the outside. "It was God's plan." "Everything happens for a reason." There are many more, but these are some of the "greatest hits." (Laughs) Now, the justification for these comments is always the same: They don't know what to say. I feel like it's time we changed the conversation from "They don't know what to say" to "Well, then let's teach them." Like the great Maya Angelou once said, "When you know better, you do better." So let's focus on the most insensitive comment of all time and the one that I feel is the most harmful: "You need to move on. Get over it! Get on with your life." Let me say this as simply as I can: When it comes to the death of someone that you love, there is no such thing as moving on. It's a lie. It's a made-up concept created by people who are too uncomfortable with death and sadness and grief. But here's the thing: It's not their fault. They are only repeating what has become familiar to them throughout the years, what's been taught to them by society over and over again. "You need to move on" is a phrase born out of centuries of ignorance and fear because grief feels a hell of a lot like fear. Now, the "move on" mentality starts very early, it's constant, and it doesn't really ever end. Within minutes of my husband's sudden death, I was attacked with questions: "Will you be donating his organs today?" "Would you like cremation or casket with that?" You know, "When can somebody come by your apartment and pick up some of his items?" Now, at the time, I was told by people that these decisions were for my benefit, that it would help me to "let go," to "put all this behind me." At my husband's funeral, a total stranger came up to me, stood right by his casket, and said to me, "Today, you grieve. Tomorrow, you get out there and find a new man!" (Laughter) Really, tomorrow? That seems a little soon! A widower friend of mine was offered this proposition from a relative of his: "For every picture you take down of you and your wife from your bedstand, I'll give you 50 bucks." A widow goes to the cemetery all the time to visit her husband, and she keeps the lawn chair in the back of her car so she can sit with him at his graveside. One day, she goes out to her car and notices her chair is gone. Her friends, thinking that they are helping, said, "We took your chair. We don't think you should go there anymore. It's not healthy." Another friend was told by her priest after her brother died in a skiing accident, "Stop talking about him; you need to let him rest in peace." Another friend: father - two sons. When he filled out a school field trip form as such, he was told by the school principal, "Your other child has died, so you only have one son now." These heart-wrenching stories are real people, and this is the kind of treatment that they face every single day. Taking away someone's connection to someone they love who has died: What purpose does that serve? What kind of message are we sending? That the people we love are replaceable? That the love you have for your daughter, your mother, your brother, your best friend has an expiration date? That their life didn't really matter? When someone you love dies and you are told over and over again to "move on," something inside of you breaks. And when that happens, you don't really feel much like living anymore. You figure, "Hey, why should I stick around when I'm not allowed to continue to love my person that I miss?" So you start isolating; you keep to yourself. Your world becomes smaller and smaller. You disappear. My friend who was offered money to get rid of pictures of him and his wife? He no longer talks about his wife anymore with anyone. He says it's just not worth it and he's tired of being silenced. The widow who had her lawn chair taken away? She still goes to the cemetery. But now she does it in secret. She tells nobody because she's tired of being judged for loving her husband who died. The woman who was told by her priest to stop talking about her brother? She did. She also stopped going to church, and her faith has suffered greatly because of it. The father that was told, "You only have one son now," was recently asked by his six-year-old boy, "Daddy, if I die like my brother did, does that mean I'm not your son anymore?" This is not the way to honor love, to honor those that we love who have died. You can't move on from love - love is the only thing that never dies. So if there's no such thing as moving on, then what is it that I'm proposing? What is the message that we need to make contagious in the hearts and minds of people, the message that will change people's minds and in turn change the world? Well, here's the truth: Love grows more love. All good things are born out of love. So what if instead of saying to someone, "Hey, stop talking about your brother," we said, "Tell me more about your brother who died"? What if instead of trying to fix people, we sat with them inside of their pain, and we let them tell us what comes next? What if we got rid of the phrase "move on" and instead began to move with and move through our losses? Imagine what could happen. Take a look. This is Ethan. At his 20-week ultrasound, he was diagnosed with developing CHD, or congenital heart disease. He wasn't supposed to make it to birth. Instead, Ethan lived seven short years of life, and in that time, he went under some of the most innovative cardiac surgeries known to date. When Ethan died, his parents, Jessica and Eric, took their forever love for him and with it created hope: The Ethan M. Lindberg Foundation offers grants, housing, support, and even music therapy to families living with CHD. Because they did not "move on" or "get over" their son, his life is now a legacy, and countless families and people are affected by his life going forward, forever. This is Philip Hernandez. Philip was a wonderful husband, father, and a great man. His life ended instantly when he was hit by a car while out cycling. That's Michele, Phil's wife. She had no plans on becoming a widow at the age of 35. After Phil's sudden death, Michele didn't know what to do, and she couldn't find the support in the community that she so desperately needed. So she built it. Michele created Soaring Spirits International, a nonprofit that connects widowed people worldwide. Their most popular program, Camp Widow, is a three-day event offering workshops, presentations, and social gatherings for widowed men and women of all ages. If Michele had listened to the countless people who told her to "let go" of her love for Phil and to "get over it," her life would have continued down the road of isolation and loneliness. Instead, her foundation has served over 3 million widowed people worldwide and counting, all because she made the choice to live and share Phil's life forward. On the right there, that's Michael. Michael is Michele's husband today. He knows that just because Michele found love with him does not mean that she is over loving Phil, nor does it mean that the pain of losing Phil has magically disappeared. He knows that the heart expands and that Michele's love for Phil is part of what makes her the great woman that he loves today. Not only does Michael support this, he's a part of it: Michael is the Camp Widow photographer. Meet Shelby. Shelby was only seven years old when her mom, Megan, died from cystic fibrosis. Shelby's dad, Mike, met Sarah at Camp Widow in Tampa, Florida. Sarah was there because her fiancé, Drew, died in a helicopter crash. Drew's parents have made the choice to continue to love Sarah as an extension of their love for their son. Megan's parents have chosen to do the same by continuing to love Mike and Shelby. Now, Shelby knows that Sarah is not her mother, but she is a mother figure, and the two have formed a really special bond. You see, Sarah was just about Shelby's age when she lost her own mother. So every time the two are around each other, their hearts heal just a little bit more. Back to this picture on the left: under the Christmas tree. It's about two weeks before Christmas. There's a knock at the door. This giant box arrives. Inside it, present after present after present from Drew's entire family - his grandparents, his parents, his aunts and uncles - all of them for Shelby, for Mike, and for Sarah. This nine-year-old little girl who had not enough time on this earth with her own mother, now giddy with joy as she opens multiple gifts from Drew's family, a man she has never even met but whose life and death is now exploding into an avalanche of love right on her living room floor. If even one person in this scenario made a different choice, this beautiful picture would not exist, and all of these people would be living much smaller lives. Instead, all of their lives grew bigger and wider. More love. The love that Mike and Sarah have for each other does not diminish or delete the love that Sarah will always have for Drew and that Mike will always have for Megan. In fact, it multiplies it, it honors it. Love grows love. And what about me? What have I done with the forever love that I'll always have for my husband, Don? Well, I'm happy to tell you I am a speaker at Camp Widow, where I've been giving my comedic presentation about life and loss, since 2013. I started a campaign called "Pay It Forward For Don Shepherd Day," where I ask people anywhere and everywhere to do acts of kindness in his honor, and then they get published in my blog. Now, over the years, hundreds of people have taken part in these acts of kindness, and many of them don't even know me, nor do they know my husband. I'm writing a book about our forever-love story and about my story after. And I'm standing here with all of you today, giving this very personal and important message into the universe. My husband's heart may have stopped beating on July 13th, 2011, but he lives on every single day because it's my mission to make damn sure of it. Great things can happen when we continue to tell the stories of those we have lost, who have died. And it doesn't have to be on this grand of a scale. Each of us can be the person that changes the message for someone else about grief, love, and loss. That is how change happens: one person, one mind at a time. Every single one of us in this room and everyone watching this online and me - guess what? We're all going to die. Not right now, so don't panic; hopefully, it's not right now. But we are - we're all going to die at some point. We have no choice about that. But guess what? We do have a choice about how we talk about those who have died, the language we use. So let me ask you this: When you die, do you want to be forgotten? Do you want people to tell your loved ones, "Hey, get over it. Get over her; get over him. Move on; stop talking about that." Or do you want the people who love you to use that love to create a life for themselves filled with joy and purpose and meaning? Isn't that what you deserve? Isn't that what we all deserve? The question that is asked in the closing song of Lin-Manuel Miranda's hit Broadway, brilliant musical "Hamilton" is this: "When my time is up, have I done enough? Will they tell my story? Will they tell your story? Who tells your story?" My wish for everyone here today, for each one of us, is that when our time is up, the people who love us never move on; the people who love us, they tell our story. Because here's the truth: If we move on and let go of and get rid of the people that we love who have died - if we do that, then guess what? They really are gone. They're gone; they're just gone forever. But if we tell each other's stories, and if we use that love to create more love and to multiply each other's worlds - if we can do that, then nobody ever really dies. Not truly. Thank you so much. (Applause)
