- At a Family Dinner, My Sister Announced She Was Moving in—Too Bad the House Wasn’t Mine Anymore: Kind: captions Language: en-US I grip my water glass tighter, knuckles whitening as Marissa's words hang in the air between us. So next week I'll be moving into your place, she says with a casual smile, as if announcing she's switching to almond milk or taking up jogging, just until I figure things out. The champagne pops open beside me, my father beaming as he pours the bubbling liquid into crystal flutes I've never seen before. My mother's china, the set she saves for Thanksgiving and Christmas gleams under the chandelier light. The table arrangement should have been my first clue, something was happening. Marissa seated at the head of the table. The formal settings. The champagne chilling. This isn't Sunday dinner. It's an ambush. I've already started packing, Marissa continues, accepting a flute from Dad with a gracious nod. It shouldn't take more than a day to get everything moved in. My parents exchange pleased glances across the table, nodding along as if this is the most reasonable plan in the world. As if my consent is merely a formality, already given. To new beginnings, my father says, raising his glass. Everyone lifts theirs in response. Everyone except me. The garage boxes make sense now. Three days ago I'd found them stacked against the wall, oversized plastic tubs labeled, Marissa's Winter Clothes and Marissa's Books. When I texted her about them, she'd brushed it off. Just temporary storage, she said, just until she found more space in her apartment. I should have known. Just like I should have known what it meant when I confronted my parents about paying Marissa's rent for the third time this year. She's going through a rough patch, Mom had said, waving away my concerns. That's what family does, we help each other. I hear my mother's voice from last week, the phone call I wasn't supposed to overhear while waiting in her kitchen. Eden is so lonely in that big house, she'd told Aunt Sarah. She needs her sister's company. It's perfect timing, really. Perfect timing. As if my life exists solely to accommodate Marissa's latest crisis. I set my water glass down carefully, reaching for my purse on the floor beside me. The trembling in my hands has nothing to do with nervousness and everything to do with fury. Why do you look so serious? Marissa laughs, her eyes darting briefly to our parents. This is good news for both of us. You get company, and I get to save on rent for a while. My fingers close around the manila envelope I'd brought the one I'd almost left in the car, thinking tonight wasn't the right time. But there is no right time with my family. There is only their time, their plans, their expectations. The tremor in my hands steadies as I place the envelope on the table. Actually, I say, my voice calmer than I feel. I have news, too. My parents' expressions shift instantly. My mother's smile falters, eyes narrowing slightly. My father lowers his champagne glass, forehead creasing with concern. The change is subtle but unmistakable, the united front forming against the daughter who isn't following the script. What kind of news? My mother's tone carries a warning. Don't ruin this. Don't be difficult. I feel the weight of the familiar labels hovering in the air. Selfish. Difficult. Uncaring. The stakes of defiance are clear. Disagree and become the family villain. Again. Looking at their expectant faces, I realize this pattern has defined my entire life. Marissa takes. I give. My parents enforce the transaction. The roles never change. The lines never shift. Until now. I place the manila envelope in the center of the table, my fingers lingering on the edge. The room falls silent, my family's anticipation hanging heavy in the air. What's that? My mother's voice carries a hint of irritation, as if my interruption is derailing an already settled plan. This, I say, carefully withdrawing the papers inside, is the sale agreement for my house. I sold it last week. I have thirty days left before I move. The silence that follows is absolute. Marissa's champagne glass slips from her fingers, shattering against the hardwood floor. No one moves to clean it up. My father's mouth opens and closes like a fish suddenly finding itself on dry land. You what? Marissa finally whispers. I flip through my phone, pulling up the Pinterest board I discovered this morning. Interesting timing, isn't it? While you were planning to move in, Mom and Aunt Sarah were already designing your future bedroom in my house. I turn the screen toward them, displaying their meticulous board labeled, Marissa's New Room. My throat tightens as I watch recognition flash across their faces. Nine years of saving every possible penny, working overtime, sacrificing vacations and new clothes all to afford that house, my dream home. And to them, it was just another resource to be redirected to Marissa. My father stands abruptly, towering over me. You can just cancel the sale, he says, waving his hand dismissively. These things have cooling off periods. My mother rises too, resting her hand on his arm. Eden, we already promised Marissa she could stay as long as she needed. You can't possibly expect her to find a place in 30 days. Actually, Aunt Sarah pipes up from the end of the table. We were going to move most of her things next Tuesday while you were at work, just to make it easier for everyone. The coordinated betrayal steals my breath. I glance at Dorothy, my mother's oldest friend, sitting uncomfortably beside Aunt Sarah. She stares fixedly at her plate, avoiding eye contact with everyone. You can't do this, my father says, his voice hardening. What about your property taxes coming due? You know I always help with those. The implied threat isn't subtle. His one annual contribution to my finances dangled now as leverage. Eden, my mother says softly, her voice taking on the concerned tone she uses when discussing unstable people. I'm worried about you. This kind of impulsive decision-making isn't like you. Are you feeling alright? Have you been taking care of yourself? I recognize the strategy immediately. If not compliant, then possibly unwell. It's a familiar path in our family dynamic, one that's kept me in line for years. You did this on purpose, Marissa accuses, tears streaming down her face. You found out I needed a place to stay and sold your house just to spite me. The accusation lands like a physical blow. This is how it always goes. My boundaries recast as cruelty, my self-preservation painted as selfishness. For a moment, I consider explaining the truth. The catastrophic roof replacement that drained my savings. The water line break. The heating system failure. The mounting financial pressure that made selling not just advisable but necessary. But what would be the point? They've already cast me as the villain in this family drama. Why give them more ammunition? I made this decision for my own reasons, I say instead, keeping my voice steady. And it's final. So you're just going to leave your sister homeless? My mother's voice rises. After everything we've done for you? The familiar weight of guilt settles on my shoulders. Everything they've done for me. The invisible ledger of family obligation that only ever seems to accumulate in one direction. Marissa isn't homeless? I point out, she has an apartment, and if that doesn't work, she has your spare bedroom. That's not the point, my father interjects. Family helps family. Period. Anyone. The unspoken remainder of that sentence hangs in the air, and you're not being family. I think, I say carefully, gathering the salepapers back into the envelope, that we have different definitions of help. Under the table, I feel a gentle squeeze on my hand. Dorothy, still avoiding eye contact with everyone else, whispers so quietly I almost miss it. Good for you. It's a small validation, but in this moment, it feels monumental. One person in this room doesn't see me as selfish or cruel for protecting myself. As I sit there with my family's accusations hanging in the air, I wonder whether I should explain the financial struggles that forced me to sell my beloved home, or simply hold firm without justifying my decision. Would sharing my vulnerability help them understand, or just give them new ammunition to use against me? The first call comes Tuesday morning while I'm fixing my coffee. Aunt Sarah's name flashes on my phone screen, unusual for a weekday before nine. Eden, honey, I just talked to your mother. Her voice carries that particular tone, the one reserved for funerals and family scandals. Is it true you sold your house just to keep Marissa from staying with you? My coffee sloshes over the rim of my mug, scalding my fingers. What? Marissa called me in tears last night, said you sold your beautiful home out of spite, knowing she needed a place to stay. The burning on my hand fades as a different heat rises in my chest. That's not what happened. Your sister will be homeless, Eden. I never thought you could be so cruel. The calls continue all day. Uncle Robert, Cousin Jennifer, even my father's golf buddy Chuck, whose connection to this situation escapes me entirely. Each conversation follows the same script. Marissa's desperate situation, my apparent heartlessness, their profound disappointment in me. While preparing dinner, I make the mistake of checking Facebook. Marissa's latest post appears at the top of my feed. The hardest lesson in life is learning who truly has your back when times get tough. Family should be forever, not just when it's convenient. 27 sympathetic comments follow, including one from Mom. We're here for you, sweetheart. Always. The knife in my hand chops carrots with increasing force. The final blow comes Thursday, when my colleague Lisa stops by my desk, her expression pinched with concern. I ran into your sister at Starbucks this morning, she says, lowering her voice. She told me about the house situation. If you need help finding her temporary housing, my church has a program. The carrot pieces on my cutting board splinter under the blade. What exactly did Marissa tell you? Lisa shifts uncomfortably. Just that she's facing homelessness because of some sudden decision you made. She seemed really upset. My jaw tightens so hard it aches. Thanks for your concern, Lisa. But Marissa isn't homeless. She has an apartment and parents with a four bedroom house. After Lisa leaves, I lock myself in the bathroom stall, hands trembling as I text my sister. Stop telling people I'm making you homeless. It's a lie and you know it. Her response arrives instantly. You're selling the house I was counting on. What would you call it? The roof replacement came first $11,000 I hadn't budgeted for when the March storms revealed leaks in three different rooms. I emptied my vacation fund and took a small loan, telling myself it was a one-time emergency. Then the waterline broke in April 8,000, $200 for emergency repairs and landscape restoration. I canceled plans to visit my college roommate in Arizona, picked up weekend marketing work for a local realtor, and ate ramen for dinner more nights than I care to admit. The heating system failure in June was the final blow $5,000 I simply didn't have. Each night after work, I'd sit at my kitchen table, surrounded by spreadsheets and bank statements, calculating and recalculating figures that refused to balance. The crying started around then. Silent tears that came only after midnight, when I was certain no neighbors walking dogs might glance through my windows and see the truth that the woman they waved to each morning, the one who always appeared composed and capable, was falling apart behind closed doors. My reflection in the bathroom mirror each morning reveals the cost dark hollows beneath my eyes that concealer can't quite hide, collarbones more pronounced against my blouses. When my pants began slipping at the waist, I punched new holes in my belt, rather than buy smaller sizes. Temporary situation, temporary solution. While maintaining this facade, I pack my home each evening. Nine years of memories sorted into keep, donate, and trash. Books that once lined entire walls now fill boxes stacked in corners. The wedding photos of friends, the graduation pictures of cousins, the vacation snapshots all bubble wrapped and nestled in containers labeled with blue marker. Through it all, I document everything. Bank statements in chronological order, house repair invoices filed by date and category, text messages screenshot and saved, a fortress of paper proof against the siege of family fiction. While packing my office bookshelf, I find it a framed photo I'd almost forgotten. Me, standing on the front porch of this house the day I signed the mortgage papers, keys clutched in my hand, smile threatening to split my face. The realtor had taken it as a surprise, catching the exact moment I realized I'd actually done it, purchased a home entirely on my own. I trace the frame's edge, remembering that feeling. Pride without a hint of apology. Accomplishment without anyone else's approval attached. I place it carefully atop the nearest keep box, this tangible reminder of what I built and what no one can diminish. The doorbell rings Saturday afternoon while I'm wrapping dishes and newspaper. Through the front window, I spot my parents' Buick in the driveway, Marissa's red compact behind it. No warning text, no courtesy call. I consider not answering, but the knocking grows more insistent. When I open the door, they file in like funeral directors, solemn, judgmental, prepared to manage a difficult situation. Mom spots the boxes immediately. You're really going through with this madness. I fold the newspaper around another plate, securing it with tape. The closing is in three weeks. Your sister needs you. Mom's voice rises slightly. You've always been selfish, but this is beyond understanding. The plate in my hands remains steady, though something shifts inside me. I've spent my entire life being told what Marissa needs. The words emerge calmer than I feel. Did anyone ever ask what I need? Dad clears his throat. Eden, be reasonable. I need financial stability. I need to sleep at night without panic attacks about bills. I need to not drain my retirement fund for house repairs I can't afford. I continue wrapping dishes methodically. Unlike past confrontations, I don't stop what I'm doing to give them my full attention. I don't offer beverages or comfortable seats. I continue my work while they stand awkwardly in my half-packed kitchen. We're your family, Mom says, voice wavering between anger and disbelief. Yes, you are. And I've never once asked you for money. Not for college, not for my first apartment, not for this house, and not for the repairs that drained my savings. For once, they have no immediate response. Marissa stares at the floor, unable to meet my eyes. Dad examines the ceiling as if architectural features have suddenly become fascinating. I realize with startling clarity that I no longer need their approval or understanding. The constant ache for validation I've carried since childhood has quieted, replaced by something sturdier. When I stand to reach for another stack of plates, I notice my posture has changed. Shoulders back, spine straight, eyes level with theirs instead of downcast as usual. The physical manifestation of an internal shift. Dorothy arrives that evening with Thai food and two bottles of wine. She helps me pack photo albums while sharing stories of her own sister, the one who didn't speak to her for three years after Dorothy refused to co-sign alone. Family patterns are the hardest to break, she says, carefully wrapping a crystal vase my grandmother left me. But sometimes breaking them is the only way forward. Monday brings an unexpected text from my colleague Thomas. Need help moving? Got a truck and strong back. Family often sees us least clearly. Just say when. That afternoon, Barbara, my financial advisor, emails confirmation of my townhouse application approval. Based on your financials, this move reduces your housing costs by 42%. The right decision for your long-term security. Call if you need anything else. The contrast strikes me as I review these messages over dinner. These people ask what I need rather than telling me what to give. They offer specific help instead of vague demands. That night, I spread my financial documents across the dining room table, bank statements, repair invoices, property tax assessments, retirement account withdrawals, everything meticulously organized, the complete picture of my financial reality. Tomorrow's family meeting won't be an intervention for me, but a reckoning with facts they've chosen to ignore. My one advantage remains unspoken. The sales agreement includes a 30-day rent-back clause. For a month after closing, I can stay in the house while finalizing my townhouse purchase. Breathing room they don't know I have. At exactly 7 o'clock Tuesday evening, the doorbell rings. When I open it, I find not just my parents and Marissa on the porch, but Aunt Sarah, Uncle Robert, and my cousin Jennifer standing behind them, a family firing squad assembled for execution. I stand at the head of my dining room table, a position that feels foreign yet right. The manila folder in my hands holds every financial statement, every bill, every painful reminder of why I had to sell my home. My fingers no longer tremble as I open it. Before anyone says another word, I need you all to see something. My voice carries a steadiness I don't entirely recognize. The family gathering looks larger than I anticipated my parents Marissa, Uncle William, Aunt Sarah, and my support team, Dorothy, Barbara, and Thomas. I lay out the documents methodically, arranging them in chronological order. Bank statements, emergency fund withdrawals, contractor invoices. Each paper tells part of the story I've kept hidden. This is the roof replacement from last spring. I slide the $11,200 invoice toward the center where everyone can see it. The 30-year shingles only lasted 22. Insurance covered nothing. Uncle William adjusts his glasses, leaning forward with a frown that cuts deep lines across his forehead. Water line repair, $8,200. Another paper joins the first. When the city updated the main line, it revealed my connection was deteriorating. No warning, no payment plan offered. NES. My mother shifts in her seat but remains uncharacteristically silent. Heating system replacement, $5,000. The third major bill completes my trinity of home ownership nightmares. The energy efficiency rebate covered barely a quarter of the cost. I maintain direct eye contact as I speak, something I rarely managed in family confrontations before. Barbara nods almost imperceptibly from her seat near the window, encouraging me to continue. My emergency fund is gone. I place my bank statement on top, the balance painfully clear. My financial advisor recommended selling while the market is still hot rather than risking a forced sale later when I might be underwater. Marissa's chair scrapes against the hardwood as she stands abruptly. This is creative accounting, Eden. You've always been good with numbers when it suits you. Her voice carries the familiar accusatory edge. You're just trying to avoid your family responsibilities. F. Uncle William, whose judgment I've feared since childhood, surprises me by turning toward my sister. Marissa, what's your current employment situation? Last I heard, you were between jobs. The unexpected redirection leaves Marissa fumbling. I'm... exploring opportunities. The job market is complicated right now. My mother jumps in. William, this isn't about employment. Family duty exists regardless of finances. Eden has always had that beautiful house with all that extra space. Speaking of space, Aunt Sarah interjects, her reading glasses perched on her nose as she examines my heating bill. Why can't Marissa stay with you and Roger? You have that guest room you just redecorated. The conversation shifts like a current, pulling away from me and swirling around the room as family members begin questioning the arrangement I've endured my entire adult life. Dorothy clears her throat. I've known this family for 20 years. Eden bought her house without any family financial support. Meanwhile, I've watched Marissa receive help with rent, car payments, and credit card bills repeatedly. Her voice carries the weight of long observation. The pattern has been clear to everyone except those closest to it. Barbara straightens her blazer before speaking. As Eden's financial advisor, I can tell you that enabling financial dependency ultimately harms both parties. The one giving depletes their resources, while the receiver never develops crucial life skills. Thomas, sitting quietly until now, leans forward. My brother lived with my parents until he was 42. They paid his bills, made his appointments, solved his problems. When they passed, he had no idea how to function. He lost their house within a year because he never learned to manage money. He glances at my parents. Sometimes the most loving thing is to stop rescuing someone. The room falls unusually quiet as these outside perspectives penetrate the family mythology we've operated under for decades. Uncle William breaks the silence. Eden, why didn't you share these financial struggles sooner? I hesitate, the question catching me off guard. Should I maintain the privacy and independence that had served as my armor for years, or would opening up to select family members create the support system I never thought possible? Before I can answer, my father shifts in his chair, eyes fixed on the hardwood floor. It's the first time I can remember him sitting through a family discussion without dominating it. My mother's rigid posture gradually softens, her hands now resting open on the table instead of clenched in her lap. Marissa stands suddenly, grabbing her purse. This is ridiculous. You're all acting like I'm some kind of parasite. Eden has always been the golden child with her perfect house and perfect job while everyone ignores how hard things have been for me. When no one jumps to her defense, she storms toward the door. The slam echoes through the house as she leaves. Uncle William waits for the reverberations to fade before speaking again. I think it's time we had a family financial planning session, for everyone. He looks pointedly toward my parents, including Marissa. My mother's eyes glisten with unshed tears, but for once, they're not being weaponized against My father reaches for her hand across the table, his face unreadable but his silence speaking volumes. For the first time in a family confrontation, I'm not the one left feeling like I've done something wrong. I arrange the last of my kitchen glasses into a box, wrapping each one in newspaper with methodical precision. My final week into my 30-day countdown, and the house already feels hollow, memories peeling away with each item packed. The doorbell rings. I check my watch right on time. My parents are nothing if not punctual when they have an agenda. I open the door to find not just my parents but Marissa too, standing on my porch like a united front. My mother clutches her purse with white knuckles. My father's smile appears rehearsed. Eat in, honey, my mother says, stepping inside without waiting for an invitation. We've been talking, and we think we've found the perfect solution. A compromise, my father adds, his voice carrying that false cheerfulness he reserves for difficult conversations. Marissa follows them in, her eyes darting around my half-packed living room before settling on me with a practiced look of gratitude. I really appreciate you hearing us out. They arrange themselves on my couch the only piece of furniture not yet wrapped in moving blankets while I remain standing. The power dynamic isn't lost on me. We know selling the house is your decision. My mother begins, punctuating her words with a nervous laugh. But instead of Marissa moving in with you, what if you helped her get her own place? My father nods enthusiastically, while carefully avoiding any mention of actual numbers, just alone to get her started. With your success, it would hardly affect you. Unlike me, Marissa interjects, her voice soft and rehearsed. Some people just have bad luck, you know? The implication hangs heavy in the air between us. You're successful. She's struggling. You owe her this. My mother leans forward, her voice dropping to a conspiratorial whisper. Eden, the family has always stuck together. If you can't help your sister just this once, she trails off, the threat clear. Cut Marissa off, and I cut myself off from the family. I walk to the dining room table where I've laid out several documents. They watch me, confusion replacing their certainty. I'm meeting with my lender tomorrow. I say, picking up a pre-approval letter, for a townhouse downtown, the monthly payment will be nearly 40% less than my current mortgage. I hand the letter to my father, whose eyes widen at the numbers. Selling this house isn't just about Marissa. It's about rebuilding my emergency savings after three major home repairs drained them. It's about reducing my financial stress. I take a deep breath. It's about finally prioritizing my financial health. My mother starts to speak, but I hold up my hand. I can't support Marissa financially, I continue, but I can help her support herself. I pull a business card from my pocket and hold it out to Marissa. She stares at it without taking it. Our marketing department has an entry-level position opening next week. The starting salary would cover rent on a one-bedroom apartment. I spoke to HR yesterday, and they'd be willing to interview you. Marissa's mouth opens, then closes. For once, she seems genuinely speechless. This isn't charity, I add. It's an opportunity. Whether you take it is your choice. My father stands abruptly, moving toward the window where bare hooks show where family photos once hung. Marissa needs... No, I interrupt, walking to the coffee table where I've placed a manila folder. This is what being reasonable looks like. I am C of no. I open the folder and pull out a spreadsheet, placing it on the table between us. The itemized list contains every financial gift my parents have given Marissa over the past five years. Car payments. Rent supplements. Credit card bailouts. The total at the bottom, circled in bright red, makes my mother gasp. I compiled this from the conversations you've had in front of me. I suspect the actual total is higher. My father picks up the paper, his forehead creasing as he studies the numbers. This isn't love, I say quietly. It's dependence, and it's hurting both of you. My father opens his mouth to object, then falls silent, his eyes fixed on the spreadsheet. We just wanted to help her, my mother whispers, tears welling in her eyes. The best help is teaching someone to stand on their own, I reply, my voice steady despite the hammering in my chest. You taught me that once, remember? When I wanted to quit college because it was too hard? The memory hangs between us, my father refusing to let me move back home, when calculus overwhelmed me, insisting I find a tutor instead. We're doing you no favors by making life too easy, he'd said then. Strange how principles change when applied to different daughters. The silence stretches, broken only by the sound of packing tape from the box I'd abandoned when they arrived. Finally, my father puts his arm around my mother's shoulders. We should go, he says quietly. They walk to the door, my mother not quite meeting my eyes. Marissa hesitates, looking back at the business card still in my hand. I don't need your charity job, she says, but there's less conviction in her voice than usual. I place the card on the entryway table. It's not charity, it's opportunity. She turns away, but as she follows my parents out, I catch her slip the card into her purse when she thinks I'm not looking. The door closes behind them. I stand in my half-empty living room, listening to their car start and pull away. The silence feels different now, not hollow, but open, expansive. I sink onto the couch, running my hand over the familiar fabric. Tomorrow it goes into storage until I move into the townhouse. Nine years of memories in this house, and now an uncertain future lies ahead. I feel both liberated and isolated, triumphant and alone. The family dinner next month looms in my mind, will they even still want me there? Has this confrontation permanently altered our relationships? The answering machine light blinks with three new messages, probably more relatives calling to weigh in. I ignore it, focusing instead on the business card Marissa took. One small step, perhaps the first of many. I stand in the center of my downtown loft, savoring the space around me. Sunlight streams through the windows, warming the hardwood floors beneath my bare feet. Three months ago, this place felt foreign, a compromise rather than a choice. Now it feels like the home I never knew I needed. Another box checked off the budget, I tell myself, updating my financial tracking app. My mortgage payment appears in green, a full 30% less than what I paid for the craftsman. Barbara's weekly check-ins have transformed what was once my greatest source of anxiety into a structured ritual I actually look forward to. The kitchen island holds my newest purchase, a professional-grade camera that would have remained a distant wish in my old financial reality. My first photography class starts tomorrow evening, the course catalog still open on my tablet. I run my fingers over the camera's textured grip, permission to create rather than just survive. My phone buzzes with a reminder, Sunday dinner at my parents. I feel the familiar tightening in my shoulders, then consciously release it. These dinners have changed, just as I have. Later, I step through my parents' front door, carrying a bottle of wine rather than my usual tension headache. Dad greets me with a hug that feels genuine instead of perfunctory. Eden, come see what your sister brought. He says, his voice carrying none of the comparative undertones that once filled our interactions. In the dining room, Marissa arranges flowers in a simple vase. She looks up, her smile reaching her eyes. They gave me my first business cards today, she says, pulling one from her pocket. Assistant marketing coordinator, sounds fancy for someone who mostly makes coffee runs, but I'm learning so much. She hands me the card proudly, without the defensive edge that once colored her accomplishments. My mother appears in the doorway, carrying a casserole that isn't placed strategically closer to Marissa's place setting. Tell us more about the social media campaign you mentioned last week. Mom says, looking directly at Marissa. Then she turns to me, and Eden, how was your photography class? I blink, still adjusting to the novelty of balanced attention. As we eat, I notice my father asking thoughtful questions of both daughters, not just fawning over Marissa's every word. One week later, laughter fills my loft as Dorothy raises her glass. To homes that shelter us and boundaries that protect us, she toasts. Around my coffee table sit Thomas, Barbara, Uncle William, and me, my chosen family, alongside the blood relative who surprised me most. Dorothy presents me with a frame containing two photos, me signing my original mortgage papers nine years ago, and me holding the keys to this townhouse. Two brave beginnings, she says softly. Two very different women, I add, recognizing how much I've changed. The following weekend finds Marissa and me at my kitchen table, surrounded by spreadsheets and calculator apps. So if I put away 15% of each paycheck, I could have first and last month's rent by January, she says, chewing her lip as she runs the numbers again. Our parents sit on my sofa, watching this scene with a mix of pride and regret. We should have done this years ago, mom admits quietly. We thought we were helping, but we were hurting both of you in different ways. Dad nods, his eyes revealing the difficult journey of unlearning decades of enabling behavior. As I watch Marissa calculate her budget with determination I'd never seen before, I wonder about the fine line between helping and enabling those we love. Is it ever possible to find the perfect balance between supporting family and maintaining healthy boundaries? Or must we sometimes allow others to struggle so they can truly grow?

- 7 Stages of a Long Distance Relationship: Kind: captions Language: en - [Amanda] Hey there Psych2Go family. We at Psych2Go are grateful and humbled by your love and support. Here at Psych2Go our mission is to make content on mental health and self-help more accessible to everyone. So, let's continue to navigate everyday psychology. (acoustic music) Research shows an estimated 75% of college students will be in a long-distance relationship at some point during university. Even though social media, messaging apps and video calls make it easy for couples to stay connected while apart for extended periods of time, long-distance relationships are anything but easy. Studies have shown higher break up rates in long-distance relationships that in close distance ones. Oh, dear! And while some long-distance relationships even start out that way when couples meet online, others transition from close distance to long-distance relationships. Here are seven stages in a long-distance relationship. One, decision. The first chapter in the book of long-distance relationships is the decision to try to make long-distance work. When one partner lives far away or is faced with an opportunity or obligation that requires travel, and the other partner cannot or chooses not to go with them, the couple has to choose between breaking up or entering into a Common factors that cause couples to consider a long distance relationship are travel due to job description or promotions, a partner in the military, education and emergency or family members moving. During the decision stage, partners evaluate whether they wanna commit to a long-distance relationship and others decided it's best to simply end their relationship. Two parting. Once both partners have agreed to pursue a long-distance relationship, so begins the second chapter titled parting. They'll try to spend every minute together before they're separated to make up for the weeks, months or years they'll spend apart. This is also the stage where partner should set boundaries and make rules to keep the relationship healthy when it transitions to long-distance. Three, transition. The transition stage comes after one or both partners have left, launching the long-distance part of their relationship. In this stage, partners might be in a state of denial or simply too busy with the practicals of moving and adjusting to life without their partner to dwell on how the relationship has changed. They'll probably message or call their partner frequently and keep them updated on every little thing, they might still feel like they're still in a close distance relationship. And then comes four, realization. Were you just about to call them to meet you for coffee? Has the fact that you can't meet up with them whenever you like just kinda sunk in? Do you feel at a loss because you could really use a hug from them right now? If so, you've now entered the next chapter, realization. During this stage, couples begin to process what life is like without their partner. They must face the reality that they can't depend on their partner to be there for them physically anymore. The realization stage, while painful also teaches partners to be more independent, have better time management and improve their communication. Five, jealousy. Did they just post a photo of them having fun at the beach with new friends? Are they planning to go to an event with their new colleagues? Like, really? Here you are not being able to even eat properly because you miss them so much while they've already made new friends. Jealousy is a common stage in long-distance relationships. In a study 125 U.S. college students who had been or were currently in long-distance relationships, concluded that jealousy was the most commonly experienced negative emotion while they were apart. Participants in the study reported feeling jealous, not only of potential romantic interest, but also a friends and family who got to spend more time with their significant other than they did. Six, doubt. Now comes the climax. Relational uncertainty is another typical stage in long-distance relationships. Long-distance partners reported that the longer they went without seeing their partner face to face the greater their relationship uncertainty became. And if one or both partners have doubts about the relationship before the transition to long-distance, their relationship has a weaker chance of surviving. And seven, validation. Validation is the last chapter in the book. Partners in a successful long distance relationship will find themselves returning to the stage again and again. While the unsuccessful ones might end in stage six, doubt, study show that the long-distance partners are just as satisfied with their relationships as close distance partners when their partner actively listen to them and responded in positive ways. This means although long-distance partners were not face to face they still felt like their relationship improved after phone or online communication, as long as they could tell their partner supported them. This study points to the importance of disclosure and open and honest conversations to build trust and validation. Long-distance relationships are high maintenance, but very rewarding. Once partners decide to commit to a long-distance relationship, they'll inevitably go through new experiences without their partner. may come with jealousy and doubts, but they also offer an opportunity for partners to step back and re-evaluate their relationship. And if they find themselves choosing each other again and again, then their relationship has a strong chance of working out. Are you in a long-distance relationship now? Have you observed these stages in such relationships around you? Do let us know in the comments below. Also remember to like and share this video with those who are or in the near future might be in a long-distance relationship. - 6 Tips on Maintaining Long Distance Relationships: Kind: captions Language: en It's popular relieved that long-distance relationships never work out But sometimes absence does make the heart grow fonder and people learn to take their partners less for granted when they're not always around It's also worth noting that some couples far away from each other feel closer than couples who live with each other Because they're more willing to communicate their problems with one another. It all boils down to one word: Effort. Here's six tips on maintaining long-distance relationships 1. Give them a personal gift to hold on to. Before the two of you depart give each other something you can hold on to and remember one another by A few examples you may be inspired by include exchanging nightlights, stuffed animals, jewelry, hoodies or mugs What you decide to give your partner doesn't have to be big or expensive Instead focus on how the object has served to bring meaning into your life. 2. Set routines and learn about each other's schedules. Time zones can be tricky, but learn to appreciate the differences. This can teach you the value of patience and remind you that relationships don't thrive or grow from moments of instant gratification Once you and your partner gets settled in and adjust to your new lifestyles, let each other know about your schedules and routines Depending on how much of a difference your time zone is You may need to take turns accommodating to each other's availability to set up Skype dates or phone calls It may sound daunting But once the two of you figure out a rhythm that works for the both of you the rest will set sail. 3. Build trust and try not to jump to conclusions or assume the worst. Life can be unpredictable. So sometimes things come up such as family emergencies, working overtime or illness that may interrupt your usual communication patterns rather than worrying about whether your partner is cheating on you or if they've grown bored of the relationship and may be spending more time with their friends know that there's no actual proof or evidence to back up those anxious thoughts. Build trust with your partner and ask each other how you're feeling, rather than bottling up insecurity and making the walls bigger Getting answers directly from your partner is better than over analyzing and filling in those gaps yourself. 4. It's not about how often you talk to each other, instead, focus on quality communication. Consistent communication is important when the two of you are apart from each other, especially when the physical aspect of the relationship is absent But too much of it can also backfire and leave the two of you feeling smothered or burnt out rather than texting each other every hour of the day, find balance and moderation and focus on the quality of your conversations instead of how frequently the two of you talk You may come to find that the more you talk to each other the more you end up talking about the same things in circles Rather than delving into a meaningful conversation that makes you appreciate each other's intellect ideas and perspectives. 5. Make time to see each other, but know that every visit may not be ideal, and that's ok. When you visit each other you may want to make the best out of your time together and plan to do exciting things But we're all human so allow room for flexibility instead of perfection There may be times when you're exhausted from traveling back and forth and just want to stay in and watch a movie with your partner Or perhaps there's a delay in your flight that may bleed into your dinner reservations Doing fun activities and bonding with your partner is important but sometimes it's good to just play things by ear and go with the flow Remember it's about the company you're with and not necessarily what the two of you do 6. Embrace the challenges together. Long distance relationships are difficult, but don't let the challenges tear you apart Instead embrace them together Sometimes your insecurities may get the best of you Consequently, you may believe the two of you would be better off breaking up and meeting new people Take a step back and think about why you held on for so long in the first place Whether the two of you are apart because you're going to different schools or because of a job promotion know that the long distance is only temporary and that you're working on yourselves before the two of you can be together again It's a common misconception to think that in order for relationships to work One person has to sacrifice their needs and desires for the other In reality, this is how relationships often break apart when people feel stifled and can't grow together Never lose sight of the big picture and don't give up! Have you ever been or are currently in a long-distance relationship? Do you have some tips on how to deal with one? Feel for to share your thoughts in the comments below If you like this video be sure to check out our website and other social media as well as our patreon account for more content And don't forget to subscribe. Thanks for watching!

with being in a long-distance relationship they no longer have as much Freedom or privacy they feel controlled they feel like they need to check in with their partner things that initially was not of being long distance forever with someone okay so let's get into some clinical observations here what have I noticed as a therapist working with largely young adults who tend to be in long-distance relationships more than older adults if neither person wants to budge on where they want to live on how they are going to close that distance between them there is very little you can do to save a relationship and I've seen the situation over and over again with couples where they're kind of figuring it out okay what are we going to do what is the future of our relationship and when they realize that neither person is willing to budge the relationship ends because there's not much else you can do it's really just a matter of delaying the breakup something like this really is an incompatibility and a deal breaker for a lot of people because like I mentioned the vast majority of people are not going to want to be far away from their partner forever I've also noticed that there's a ton of power struggle between deciding who gets to move where there's a power struggle associated with the decision itself but the decision itself also reflects a lot of the power dynamics in the relationship so if one person tends to call all the shots and the other is more submissive that becomes apparent in the way that they manage this turning point when there's someone in a relationship ship who is very stubborn and unwilling to compromise that reflects in the long distance relationship decision you know that person is probably not going to move where the other partner is but it's not the location itself that's the problem it's this person's stubbornness and you might want to be so important to your partner that they're willing to make that sacrifice for you so it can build a lot of resentment if you find out that that's not the case especially if you were usually the one bending over backwards trying to accommodate them and then they're not willing to do this big decision either I do think that the distance can prolong that idealization phase that we associate with like the honeymoon phase but again remember that that idealization doesn't always result in long-term commitment but when it does boy does it feel awesome so what are the takeaways from this literature your relationship is not doomed to fail if you are in a long-distance relationship you have the same chance of making it as non-long-distance relationships do and if you have this belief that long distance relationships don't last you might be shooting yourself in the foot you might be setting yourself up for a self-fulfilling profit scene your attitude on this matters way more than the objective statistics it matters more than how far away you are from your partner or who moves where when along the same lines if your partner has this aversion to long-distance relationships or this beliefs that they don't last or just personally doesn't want to be in one it's probably not worth it to try to convince them because they are that person that is most likely to not last in a long-distance relationship if you are trying to decide whether to move to different locations for a short or long period of time don't try to convince them if they say that they're not going to be happy in one they're probably not going to be happy in one so either try to not be far apart or just break up and I've witnessed this firsthand in my personal life with people that say you know I don't want to be in a long-distance relationship and it can feel a little bit heartbreaking for the partner that's hearing that because of course everyone wants to feel like they're so special to their partner that their partner would make that sacrifice for them but honestly it's not about you it's about them because I've seen this with couples who intend to even marry each other who one person says to the other no like I'm not going to be in a long-distance relationship with you if you move to willing to make that sacrifice for you I'm sure that you can find someone who will so just keep that in mind do try to get as much face-to-face time as possible and to visit each other frequently a He justified gruesome experiments on live animals, arguing the cries of apparent agony were no more than automatic responses. Descartes believed only humans had mind or for him it was a spiritual thing. Only man had the spirit given by God that exists independently from the body. It was these transcendent minds or spirits that Descates believed gave humans the unique capacity of self-awareness and intelligence. So that he could say probably the most famous line in Western philosophy, "I think therefore I am." These ideas created splits in how we understand ourselves, one between our minds and our bodies, that we are our minds or spirits inner body separate from it, and a second split between the human and nature. If it's only us that have minds or spirits, then it's only us that are intelligent or sacred. Our bodies, the animals nature then are not intelligent, inferior, automatic. Science, in the subsequent years, they got rid of the spirits and the sacredness, but they still kept this idea that rational thinking minds are the only intelligence in the universe, which gave us a feeling of isolation, that we are this blip of intelligence facing an otherwise not intelligent, automatic universe. Where does that voice in our heads fit into who we are? I wanna show you something close to the mountain, an ancient burial ground that can help us understand the origins of the human mind. This is a burial ground made in the Bronze Age and it's clear that people that made it here 4,000 years ago had brains capable of complex thinking. They could create stories that motivated the community to come together to create this structure. Now, here's where things get quite mind boggling. We found evidence to say that's not just our species that had minds capable of storytelling. This is a human skull. This skull looks human, but then the eyebrows are really far forward and the whole thing is shaped like a rugby ball. This is not a skull of a Homo sapien but a Homo neanderthal. It's easy to forget that there have been over 20 species of human that have lived in our planet. What were these humans like? (gentle music) The Neanderthals lived in community. They made their own clothes out of animal hides, they made tools to help them hunt, and they wore feathers for decoration. And some of the skeletons we found had been laid in dug pits, with the person's hands placed supporting their heads as if they were resting asleep. The sites we found did not just have one skeleton, but many bodies, people of different ages - children, parents, grandparents, some bodies surrounded by antler horns and rhinoceros skulls. The Neanderthals buried their dead with care and in community. Why? What the burials we found tell us about what minds the Neanderthals had, and so where our minds might come from isn't clear cut. You don't actually need complex ideas about life after death to motivate ritual behaviour. Take elephants for example, when one of their troop passes away, they will come back to the body for many days after the death, each member spending time in silence, touching the body, showing their complex emotional life, experiencing what we would likely call grief. The Neanderthals too, when their loved ones passed away, would've felt intense emotions, heartache, rolling tension in the stomach. And maybe it was these emotions alone that motivated them to bury their loved ones with care. They didn't need to have ideas about what might happen after death. So, we need more evidence. And in 2013, archaeologists in France discovered something that shocked the world. 350 metres deep into a cave, they found hundreds of stalactites and stalagmites that had been broken off the cave walls and deliberately placed into large circles. And they found the remains of fires that had once been lit within them. The structure was so complex, the archaeologists assumed it must have been made by Homo sapiens, but when they dated the find, they found it to be an astonishing 174,000 years old, long before Homo sapiens had reached Europe, implying its makers were the Neanderthals. What they found in that cave in France had no obvious purpose. It must have taken many hours to make the fires around it, the whole cave would've been choking with smoke. It seems to me very likely that there was a story motivating it, that that structure meant something to the Neanderthals. And even if that story was just made with basic language or images arising in the minds of the Neanderthals, that would mean that I think therefore I am is not just for us Homo sapiens. Descartes believed our thinking minds made us superior to the rest of nature, but we found evidence that the Neanderthals felt and thought in ways surprisingly similar to us, and we found the animals experience rich emotions and navigate their lives creatively. We haven't found any splits between humanity and nature, or between our minds and bodies, so us asking where does our intelligence come from has led us to a bigger and deeper question. How is it that our planet is bursting with beings that are conscious, having experiences and so are able to solve the challenges they face intelligently? What consciousness is or how conscious life began on planet Earth is a great mystery. Planet Earth was once just rocks and water, but what we can see from the fossil record is that life on earth began billions of years ago and through evolution became more and more complex. Eventually animals evolved and they began to experience emotions such as fear and these emotions became more and more complex like care for family or the desire to play. And eventually for some animals, thoughts and basic language began to arise in their minds, giving them the ability to interpret and tell stories about what they were experiencing. And for one animal, us Homo sapiens, we developed highly advanced language, which led to an explosion of human power and lots of questions. Every human culture has some form of spirituality or religion, stories to help satisfy our natural curiosity. We want to understand who we are and what we are a part of. Given the infinite mystery we find ourselves involved with, any understanding of ourselves needs to be made with humility. As the Bible says, "With humility comes wisdom." But we found we can through observation, determine some of the principles that shape us and the universe. Science at its core is this process of observation, making predictions about the world, collecting evidence, seeing what the evidence tells us. Science doesn't have to feel cold and mechanical. In fact, it's led us to an understanding that many wisdom traditions shared, something that's truly inclusive and universal. That all of us, no matter our religion, nationality, gender, or even our species, we all come from the same mysterious source. One of the great barriers to us finding peace is disconnection. And so if you have thoughts telling you that you are separate from the world, that there's nothing inspiring about you, shift your focus and reconnect with what you are a part of. You're made from the same energy that created the many humans, the plants, the animals, and our precious, beautiful home, planet Earth. You are embodied awareness, mind and body coming from the same mysterious source. And we found you have two main ways of experiencing and understanding yourself. The first we called the sensor, the complex array of that gave us this feeling of being. And then there's what we could call the storyteller that which watches what you experience and, using thoughts, narrates and interprets, and creates stories about who you are. And to really understand how the storyteller works can be life changing. So to demonstrate how this works, I'm going to look a wee bit silly for you. So, each ball is a thought like my name is George. I grew up in England. My love language is vegan brownies. I know all the words of the Tarzan soundtrack off by heart. And see the story of who we are, we construct from thoughts that pop into our heads that we identify with. Would you like to meet your storyteller? What's your favourite thing to do? What was that thought you just had? There you go, there's one of yours. And if we really understand this it leads to something tragic that we can only laugh at, that there's nothing intrinsically George about me. If I was born in another country, I'd have different thoughts, I'd have a different story. And in the various times of my life where I have felt not good enough, it's not like not good enough is a physical quality of the universe. No, that feeling came from judgmental thoughts that popped into my head that I identified with. So isn't it astonishing the amount of pain that we create in our lives from the stories that are only true to the extent that we identify with, and believe the thoughts that pop into our heads? I remember first reading classic spiritual books like "The Power of Now" and finding Taoism, the philosophy of the mountain where I was studying that I have since dedicated a lot of my life to. I remember these insight moments. Whoa, I'm not just my thoughts and not every thought I have needs to be true. Instead of fighting my thinking, I began to just pause and to be curious., noticing these stories created a space within for me to be able to choose my response. And a beautiful way that we can bring the practice of the pause into our every day is with a mindful breath. I do this throughout the day, and if you'd like to join me, why don't we take a breath together, breathing in together, softening the face and breathing out, letting any tension go. Let's try one more of those. Breathing in. Can you soften even more? And breathing out, letting any tension go. If we notice painful thinking of stories, we just pause, bring some curiosity to it, open some space within, and that space is our freedom. So we were promised by the great teachers, And indeed, we found we are not just our thoughts, not every thought that we have is true. We found the practice of the pause, all important steps on the journey towards peace. But inevitably, even if we have insight today, maybe tomorrow we'll be back, covered in takeaway grease, scrolling on TikTok, wondering, "How did I get here again?" So just knowing who we are isn't enough. So I've got my ice axe, crampons in the bag, I'm cold and we haven't even started yet. It's time for us to climb back up the mountain. Can the perspective it gives us help us understand how to transform what we've learned into wisdom that can actually help us? Where is this peace we've been promised? We found that we create our own stories through the thoughts Why is it then if we do create our own stories, we tell such rubbish negative stories. Why is inner peace so hard? (water bubbling) So if I draw a line in the sandy rocks, it's not gonna stay around for very long. If I scoop something a bit deeper, it could be around for a bit longer, give water enough time, and it can carve canyons out of sheer rock. Our brains work like this, you know, about neural pathways. The more you repeat certain behaviours, the more that neural pathway is strengthened. And this is essential, it gives us our habitual ways of thinking and being. Take the habit of worry for example, it's a good idea for safety for us to sometimes worry about what might go wrong in the future. The challenge comes with what we do with these habits. So for me, my time of crisis, a new character began to dominate my storytelling headspace, who I personified as a little evil wizard called the Underminer. And the Underminer channelled everything that was happening in my life through the habit of worry and negativity. So the fact that I was single and didn't have many friends, my Underminer would tell me I was flawed goods. And when I started getting anxious, my Underminer would tell me I was weak for being anxious. More and more of my life flowed into Underminer Valley until I lost all hope that I could feel better again. (ominous music) Rule number one of getting out of the hole - stop digging. How do we normally react to pain? I was beating myself up. Maybe we numb ourselves out or try and run away from ourselves. But all this does is just multiplies the pain that we're feeling. Wisdom traditions around the world all talk about the power of love and compassion for us to be hurting in this often chaotic world, doesn't make us weak or flawed. It's okay to not be okay. With compassion, can we extend love and kindness to our whole selves, even the bits that we might not want? We can be with pain instead of lost in it. The acceptance of where we are is crucial on the journey towards peace, but we don't have to be stuck in Underminer Valley forever. There are more empowering ways of responding to the challenges of life, we just need to find them. The word wisdom derives from the root to see. It's no coincidence that mountains around the world have been spiritual places. With perspective, we see that we can choose how to respond to life. Look, there's Underminer Valley, it still has its place. There's Play Valley, there's Creativity Creek. Wisdom is when we channel what happens in our lives towards the responses that serve harmony, that serve peace. And any way, we're all unique, so your place of perspective will be different to mine and the stories that empower you, you need to discover for yourself. But be empowered - with patience, persistence and kindness, change, big or small, is possible. Even if you think you've found who you are, found a story that feels good, when the world changes and your old patterns start rumbling in, how do you respond? Left it quite late with the filming and the weather's turning. It's getting cold. Still got quite a way to go before we get to safety and warmth. Getting cold. We'll be all right, we'll get through. Change can be scary, which is why we crave certainty. Certainty about how the world works, certainty about who we are. So we think we've got everything under control, but the only constant is change. The weather on the mountain is constantly changing, you're constantly changing, I'm constantly changing. Can we trust the intelligence of the life force that animates us? An intelligence that has produced the astonishing resilience that is you that has produced the breathtaking beauty of the planet. All done without words. Finding yourself is great, a human being needs a story. We have roles to play as parents, children, employees, students, citizens, but know that the searching will never end. Can you also develop the subtle art of losing yourself, to surrender to the ever-changing flow? To not need a story, to embrace the mystery of your being and so rest? On our journey towards understanding ourselves and living in peace, we found that we create the stories of who we are from the thoughts that pop into our heads, and so we have the freedom to choose a story that empowers. But, we and the world, are always changing and too complex to fully capture with words. Thankfully, we don't always need to be thinking, we can hang out in our sensations and so lose ourselves. And one of the most reliable ways I've found to lose myself is being in nature. When I feel stuck in self-criticism and painful stories, if I can get out and walk in landscapes bigger than myself, I'm reminded of the great tapestry of life that I am a part of. And George and all my stories feel less and less important. As my mind softens, I find moments of no thought. George disappears. And what remains is a simple presence, ease and connection. Mountains have been great teachers in my life and so if you can get out and have some adventures, go for it. But I know you may not have access to mountains or fancy kit like this. What we've learned can only truly be impactful if it helps us back in the mystifying mountains of modern living. It's time for me to go home, back to the city. The intelligence of the universe has achieved some pretty awesome stuff. It's easy to forget, but all of our technology is still nature expressing itself. There's much to celebrate about humanity, but at the same time, our modern world brings with it big challenges. I've been back in the city for a few months now, and the challenging bit is living with wisdom, living in peace. And if that resonates with you, we're not alone with that. To struggle is to be human, which is why the wisdom traditions have always offered practices. Practices to help us stay in connection with our wiser selves. And I want to share with you a practice that has been really helpful for me but first... A ghost story. Mwahaha! No, not really. If we imagine that our consciousness is like a light, our awareness illuminates that which it focuses on, and we've been shining the light of our awareness and the world and ourselves. But it's hard to live what we've been learning because for some reason the default setting of human consciousness is this - We're flitting between craving and distractions the whole time, which means that we stay unconscious. And it's only if we can learn to steady our awareness, to rest, that we see ourselves clearly and so see our potential for wisdom and for peace. And this brings us to meditation. Meditation is a big word, but it's about presence. If your awareness is on what you're doing as opposed to lost in thought, that's meditation. And meditation as a practice is training our awareness to rest, to find that presence more easily. Meditation has been discovered all around the world over history. It's helped me so much and I thought maybe we could try some together, so you can experience for yourself how you don't always have to be caught in thought, how you can lose yourself, how you can rest, how to stay in connection with your wisdom to help you live in peace in your every day. So would you like to join me for a meditation? If you would, then you can be sitting in any way you'd like to. And an invitation, and this is only an option and taking as long as you want with it, is for you to close your eyes and to see if you can find your breath. Where is your breath right now? Maybe coming in and out through your nostrils, maybe out your mouth? And can you slow your breath down? Slow your breath down. Softening the face. If there's any tension in your forehead or your eyes, allow them to unwind. If there's any tension in your cheeks or your jaw, allow them to sink. The sensation of your breath is part of your sensor. That feeling of being. You're not just your thoughts and so you can rest in your sensations. And so can you kindly be watching your breath coming in and out. And you may also notice thinking, that's your storyteller in action, and thinking, like the sensation of your breath, is an experience arising in your awareness. You're not just your thoughts. Not every thought that you have is true. You are the embodied awareness that watches it all. And so you have the freedom when you notice thinking to just let it go, to bring your awareness back to the breath. Meditation is not about having a quiet mind. Instead, it is the process of returning. We get lost in thought and we come back. And how gently can you come back? Can you come back without judgement? Can you rest with the breath? If we use meditation to improve ourselves, then we're back in thinking and stories, and they were precisely the things that got us confused. So can you hold this openness, this space for its own sake, with no expectations of magic fixes or future rewards? Nothing to do, nowhere to go, no one to be. Can you lose yourself? Can you rest? And gently beginning to come back, bringing some movement into your shoulders, some shoulder rolls, and then taking it as long as you want, beginning to come back and opening your eyes. It's a real privilege to be practicing with you. And for me, doing a daily meditation practice has been training in my awareness to find that kindness, to find that spaciousness more easily. Imagine how different your life would be if you could bring that space and kindness into your everyday? In an argument, instead of being stuck in the valleys of reactivity, blame, what would happen if you remembered to pause? Take a breath to find your inner mountain view and to look with kind curiosity, what feelings and stories are you in? What might be happening for the person in front of you? Or take something as mundane as your daily commute. If you apply the practice of the pause, find your inner mountain view, you may begin to notice that nature, even in the city, makes its living and you reconnect with what you're a part of, and yet we so easily forget. So with practice, it is possible for us to feel resilient, even joyful, but we still are missing something because even if we become master meditators and discover our inner chill, if our loved ones are struggling or our country's at war or we're facing climate disasters, it's going to be hard for us to live in peace. And that's because true peace is not just peace with ourselves, it's peace in our relationships, communities, countries, the planet. We've been exploring this idea And if that means true peace, this leads us to a big question - could it be that understanding who we really are can not only transform our lives, but change the world? there's a little ritual uh I picked up that I still Implement to this day and it was simple at the very end of my workout or my run I would always add 22 seconds my lucky number twice two and two so habit sort of evolved to take on a life of its own and would come to symbolized for me uh a little challenge the idea that the end is never really the end and no matter how tired I was or how bad I wanted to stop especially when you can see that Finish Line in reach I could always squeeze out a little more like there's always something extra to give and you could certainly go down that 22 second - What Challenges Do Remote Workers Face When Relocating to Asia? | Living in Asia as an American News: Kind: captions Language: en What challenges do remote workers face when relocating to Asia? In recent weeks, many American remote workers have been considering relocating to Asia. This trend is gaining traction as more people seek new experiences while working from anywhere. However, several challenges come with this move. Understanding these obstacles can help those planning to make the transition. First, the work environment in Asia can be quite different. Many Asian economies are currently experiencing stagnant productivity levels. This situation can impact job expectations and work culture. Remote workers may need to adjust their work styles to fit in with local practices. Next, the cost of living varies significantly across the continent. For instance, cities such as Singapore and Tokyo have a high cost of living. In contrast, countries like Vietnam and Thailand offer a more affordable lifestyle. Remote workers should carefully research their expenses to maintain their desired quality of life. Health care is another critical factor. The quality of health care systems varies widely in Asia. Countries like Japan and South Korea have advanced health care options. However, other nations may have limited access to quality care. It is essential for Americans relocating to ensure they have adequate health insurance that covers international needs. Housing can also pose a challenge. Finding suitable accommodations in densely populated cities may requiring extensive research. Remote workers should explore neighborhoods and housing options well in advance to secure affordable living arrangements. Cultural and social adjustments can be difficult as well. Adapting to local customs and building a social network is vital for a smooth transition. Remote workers should take the time to understand and appreciate the culture of their new home. Language barriers can be significant in some countries. Learning basic phrases in the local language can make everyday tasks much easier. This effort can enhance communication and help remote workers feel more at home. Lastly, the logistics of relocating can be complex. Moving belongings and navigating visa requirements often take considerable time and planning. Seeking professional help can simplify this process and reduce stress. As remote work becomes more common, understanding these challenges is essential for anyone considering relocating to Asia. With the right preparation, American remote workers can successfully navigate this exciting journey.
