You can download&nbsp; that PDF with the link in the description.&nbsp; All right. Let's get started with our first very&nbsp; American thing that almost all Americans know what&nbsp;&nbsp; it is. If I asked you, "What are you doing&nbsp; for the fourth," would you know what I mean?&nbsp;&nbsp; You might look at your calendar and say, "November&nbsp; 4th? December 4th?" No. When someone asks you,&nbsp;&nbsp; "What are you doing for the fourth," they&nbsp; always mean July 4th. And that's because&nbsp;&nbsp; July 4th is the American Independence Day. And&nbsp; we usually just shorten this to the fourth.&nbsp;&nbsp; "What are you doing for the fourth?&nbsp; I'm going to the lake for the fourth.&nbsp;&nbsp; I'm not really doing anything for the fourth,&nbsp; but maybe I'll try to find some fireworks."&nbsp;&nbsp; We often shorten Independence Day or July 4th&nbsp; to just the fourth. And now you know that too.&nbsp; If I said to you, "Whoa, I just found a&nbsp; Benjamin on the street. I can't believe it."&nbsp;&nbsp; Huh? Would you think that I found a guy named&nbsp; Benjamin on the street and I just feel so excited?&nbsp;&nbsp; Maybe he's my long lost friend. Nope. If I said I&nbsp; found a Benjamin on the street, that means a $100&nbsp;&nbsp; bill. And that's because Benjamin Franklin is&nbsp; the guy who's on the $100 bill. So if you say,&nbsp;&nbsp; "Oh man, I really just need to earn some Benjamins&nbsp; this weekend. I need to pay my rent. I'm falling&nbsp;&nbsp; behind." That means you need to make several&nbsp; hundred dollars. It's just slang for a hundred&nbsp;&nbsp; dollars. So I hope for you, you find a Benjamin&nbsp; on the street someday. That would be amazing.&nbsp; If my husband Dan said, "Did you eat&nbsp; the last piece of cake?" I might say,&nbsp;&nbsp; "I plead the fifth." What is happening here? Am&nbsp; I telling him that I'm guilty? Or am I telling&nbsp;&nbsp; him I'm not guilty? I didn't do it. Well,&nbsp; this expression I plead the fifth has to do&nbsp;&nbsp; with the fifth amendment to the constitution.&nbsp; And that means that I don't need to tell you&nbsp;&nbsp; because maybe it will hurt me in a court of law&nbsp; later. This is a very legal type of statement.&nbsp;&nbsp; But we often use this in daily conversation&nbsp; to say yes, but I don't want to tell you.&nbsp; So when my husband asks me, "Did you eat the last&nbsp; piece of cake," and I say, "I plead the fifth,"&nbsp;&nbsp; he knows, yes, Vanessa ate it,&nbsp; but she doesn't want to tell me.&nbsp;&nbsp; So you can use this very American&nbsp; expression when you want to tell someone&nbsp;&nbsp; yes, but maybe you feel embarrassed to tell&nbsp; them yes directly. You can just say, "Ah,&nbsp;&nbsp; I plead the fifth." Great expression. In 2004, I was a senior. Does this mean I was old&nbsp;&nbsp; in 2004? What if I told you also that in 2008,&nbsp; I was also a senior. Huh? If you're in America,&nbsp;&nbsp; you can probably guess that this means I was in&nbsp; high school in 2004 and I was in college in 2008.&nbsp;&nbsp; A senior means you are in your last year of high&nbsp; school or your last year of college. So in the US,&nbsp;&nbsp; we often have terms for first, second, third, and&nbsp; fourth year in high school and in college. So if&nbsp;&nbsp; you are in ninth grade, you are a freshman. If you&nbsp; are in 10th grade, you're a sophomore. Notice that&nbsp;&nbsp; the O in the middle of this word is silent,&nbsp; sophomore. If you're in 11th grade, you are&nbsp;&nbsp; a junior. We could say, "I'm in my junior year.&nbsp; I'm a junior." And if you're in two 12th grade, so&nbsp;&nbsp; the final grade of high school, you are a senior. And this type of system also applies to college.&nbsp;&nbsp; You are a freshman, sophomore, junior, and&nbsp; senior in college, especially when you go to&nbsp;&nbsp; a four year college, these type of terms apply.&nbsp; So it's quite common that when you are a senior,&nbsp;&nbsp; you think you are much better than freshman. "Oh,&nbsp; I was never so small. They're so much like just&nbsp;&nbsp; little children, those freshmen. We are seniors."&nbsp; I think this is pretty common in high school&nbsp;&nbsp; that you see the people who are younger than&nbsp; you as so much younger. "I wasn't like that&nbsp;&nbsp; when I was a freshman." But really, you&nbsp; were. So this type of system is quite&nbsp;&nbsp; common in the US. And now you know about it too. When you arrive in the US, you will probably need&nbsp;&nbsp; to fill out a visa form. And at the bottom of&nbsp; the visa, you need to sign your John Hancock.&nbsp;&nbsp; Is your name John Hancock? Well, if&nbsp; it is, you definitely need to do that.&nbsp;&nbsp; But this term, sign your John Hancock just means&nbsp; your signature. There's a little bit of American&nbsp;&nbsp; history behind this because John Hancock&nbsp; was a guy who signed his name big and large&nbsp;&nbsp; on the Declaration of Independence. So it was&nbsp; kind of a bold statement where these colonists&nbsp;&nbsp; in the United States or in the Americas told&nbsp; England, "We want to have independence." And&nbsp;&nbsp; it's a pretty big statement. Maybe there's some&nbsp; treason involved. So it's kind of scary to say,&nbsp;&nbsp; "I'm someone who supports this." But John Hancock signed his name&nbsp;&nbsp; really large on that document. So it was bold and&nbsp; courageous. It was a big move. So because of this,&nbsp;&nbsp; his name is synonymous now with a signature. Sign&nbsp; your John Hancock, put your John Hancock here. We&nbsp;&nbsp; don't always use this instead of signature, but&nbsp; you will definitely hear this. And it's steeped&nbsp;&nbsp; in American history and now you can use it too. Now we have two fun American idioms. One is if I&nbsp;&nbsp; said to you, "You shouldn't buy a new car. If you&nbsp; buy a used car, you get more bang for your buck."&nbsp;&nbsp; This expression, bang for your buck, kind of&nbsp; includes two American concepts. One is buck,&nbsp;&nbsp; which is slang for dollars. And the other one,&nbsp; bang, doesn't necessarily relate to guns, but&nbsp;&nbsp; I know guns are kind of culturally linked to the&nbsp; US. So we get this idea that you can get a great&nbsp;&nbsp; value for your money if you buy a used car. So&nbsp; you might say, "Yeah, you should get a used car.&nbsp;&nbsp; It's more bang for your buck. Don't get a new&nbsp; car. It's better to get a used car." This is a&nbsp;&nbsp; great expression, more bang for your buck. When I was growing up, before we left for&nbsp;&nbsp; school almost every morning, it was pretty&nbsp; common for my sister and I to fight over&nbsp;&nbsp; who said, "Shotgun," first. Why were we trying&nbsp; to beat each other to say shotgun? What's that&nbsp;&nbsp; mean? Does that mean we want to carry a shotgun?&nbsp; Does that mean we were going hunting or shooting?&nbsp;&nbsp; No. Instead, shotgun is the passenger seat. And&nbsp; for some reason, this is the most desirable seat&nbsp;&nbsp; in the car when you're not the driver. So kids,&nbsp; I guess, not too young, but high schoolers or&nbsp;&nbsp; older kids always want to call shotgun. And&nbsp; we use that expression, "I call shotgun,"&nbsp;&nbsp; or just saying shotgun. And that means that's&nbsp; my seat. I claim it. And this is really common,&nbsp;&nbsp; especially with siblings, even with friends,&nbsp; too, that before you get in the car, if you're&nbsp;&nbsp; not the driver, you'd say, "I call shotgun."&nbsp; Well, maybe now you can call shotgun too.&nbsp; Have you ever eaten the most tasty bonfire treat&nbsp; in the entire world? This is something I look&nbsp;&nbsp; forward to every time we have a bonfire. It is&nbsp; s'mores. S'mores. This is something I didn't&nbsp;&nbsp; realize was very American until I was talking&nbsp; to some international friends. And they said,&nbsp;&nbsp; "What? We just toast marshmallows. What do&nbsp; you put with them?" So let me explain to you&nbsp;&nbsp; a delicacy, something amazing that&nbsp; has a special place in my heart.&nbsp; First, you have a graham cracker. This is a&nbsp; special type of cookie, even though it's called&nbsp;&nbsp; cracker, it's quite sweet. And then we have a&nbsp; piece of chocolate. So we often buy some Hershey's&nbsp;&nbsp; chocolate, break off a piece, put it on the graham&nbsp; cracker. And when you're having a bonfire, you set&nbsp;&nbsp; that close to the fire, not too close, but close&nbsp; enough that the chocolate gets a little soft. And&nbsp;&nbsp; then you take a stick and two marshmallows, you&nbsp; could do one, but two is better, and you toast&nbsp;&nbsp; them over the fire, very carefully to make sure&nbsp; that they don't explode into flames. Some people&nbsp;&nbsp; like that. Personally, I like them to be perfectly&nbsp; toasted and it is an art form. Let me tell you.&nbsp; So you toast them. And then when they're toasted,&nbsp; you put them on the chocolate and you take another&nbsp;&nbsp; piece of a graham cracker and you slide the&nbsp; marshmallows off and there you have the perfect&nbsp;&nbsp; s'more. Toasty, warm, melty, gooey. So perfect.&nbsp; So the next time that you are at a bonfire,&nbsp;&nbsp; maybe you can introduce this idea to your&nbsp; friends, or if you're at a bonfire in the US,&nbsp;&nbsp; you will definitely see people eating s'mores.&nbsp; Please try it for my sake. I hope you'll love it.&nbsp; All right. Now it's time for the song&nbsp; you have been waiting for. It is ...&nbsp;&nbsp; Do you know this song? This song is Take Me Out&nbsp; to the Ball Game. And I included this here because&nbsp;&nbsp; there's a funny little story I wanted to share&nbsp; with you. One of my friends, you might follow his&nbsp;&nbsp; YouTube channel. His name's Jack. He has a YouTube&nbsp; channel called To Fluency and he's from the UK,&nbsp;&nbsp; but he's married to an American. And he told me&nbsp; that one time they were playing a game with some&nbsp;&nbsp; Americans. And the game was that you had to hum&nbsp; a song and your partner had to guess what it was.&nbsp; So his wife drew a card and it said, hum&nbsp; Take Me Out to the Ball Game. And she's like,&nbsp;&nbsp; "All right, this is a good song. It's easy. He'll&nbsp; understand it immediately." So she started to hum.&nbsp;&nbsp; And he just looked at her like, "What? I've never&nbsp; heard that in my life." So obviously that was not&nbsp;&nbsp; a good moment in their game for them, maybe a&nbsp; little funny, but because of this, I realized,&nbsp;&nbsp; "Oh, maybe this song isn't quite so well known&nbsp; internationally, but it is a staple of American&nbsp;&nbsp; culture because baseball is extremely common.&nbsp; And this song is sung before every baseball game.&nbsp; So if you've ever been to any kind of big baseball&nbsp; game in the US, you've definitely heard this song.&nbsp;&nbsp; And if you memorize the lyrics, you'll be&nbsp; able to sing along and cheer with everyone.&nbsp;&nbsp; It's a great moment to kind of have that group&nbsp; feeling of excitement before a game. So make&nbsp;&nbsp; sure you check out Take Me Out to the Ball Game.&nbsp; You can search on YouTube and see some clips of&nbsp;&nbsp; crowds of people at baseball games, singing&nbsp; this song, quite fun and very American.&nbsp; Take me out to the ballgame. Take me out to the&nbsp; crowd. Buy me some peanuts and cracker jacks.&nbsp;&nbsp; I don’t care if I ever get back&nbsp;&nbsp; for it root, root, root for the home team.&nbsp; If they don’t win it’s a shame. For it’s 1,&nbsp;&nbsp; 2, 3 strikes you’re out at the old ball game! Our final very American thing today also has to do&nbsp;&nbsp; with baseball. What if I said to you, "Your speech&nbsp; was awesome. You knocked it out of the park."&nbsp;&nbsp; Did you give a speech about baseball? Are&nbsp; you playing baseball? No. This just means&nbsp;&nbsp; it was amazing. You did even better than&nbsp; expected. You knocked it out of the park.&nbsp; Here we have the image of someone playing&nbsp; baseball. He's got a baseball bat,&nbsp;&nbsp; someone's pitching him the ball and he hits it.&nbsp; He doesn't just hit it into the outfield. That's&nbsp;&nbsp; where someone can catch it. He hits it beyond the&nbsp; field, out of the park. And this means it's a home&nbsp;&nbsp; run. No one can catch it. And it's great for his&nbsp; team. Everyone can get to home base and they might&nbsp;&nbsp; get a lot of points. So here knocking it out of&nbsp; the park is a great thing in baseball and in life.&nbsp; So if you do something really awesome, better than&nbsp; expected, you can use this expression. "Oh man,&nbsp;&nbsp; I can't believe it. But I talked to someone&nbsp; in English the other, and I think I knocked&nbsp;&nbsp; it out of the park. I explained myself exactly the&nbsp; way I wanted. I did it. And they understood me."&nbsp;&nbsp; Congratulations, you knocked it out of the park. Well, congratulations. - The 4 Stages of Adaptation [Culture Shock]: Kind: captions Language: en When we study in another country or move&nbsp; into a new town, we are often exposed to&nbsp;&nbsp; a culture different from ours and need&nbsp; to go through four stages of transition: Honeymoon, frustration,&nbsp; adjustment, and adaptation. During stage two, some experience a real&nbsp; culture shock, severe forms of which can&nbsp;&nbsp; lead to reactions that may need medical&nbsp; attention. But before we come to that,&nbsp;&nbsp; let’s first look at what happens typically. In stage 1 — honeymoon — everything is beautiful.&nbsp; The people are friendly, the culture rich, and the&nbsp;&nbsp; way of local life fascinating. We are optimistic&nbsp; and excited about all the differences. In stage 2 we feel frustration. Reality&nbsp; sets in. We have come to understand that&nbsp;&nbsp; there are plenty of barriers to connecting&nbsp; with people and have difficulty with the&nbsp;&nbsp; social norms. We start to reject local&nbsp; traditions and begin to feel homesick. The stage of adjustment is&nbsp; characterized by a growing&nbsp;&nbsp; familiarity with our new way of life.&nbsp; We begin to understand the new customs,&nbsp;&nbsp; acquire language skills, and make local&nbsp; friends. We start to feel more comfortable. Last is adaptation. During this stage, we&nbsp; gain a sense of belonging and are able to&nbsp;&nbsp; fully participate in the new culture, even while&nbsp; maintaining our own native identity. Known as the&nbsp;&nbsp; stage of mastery, we now start to feel at home.&nbsp; However, not everyone arrives at this point. Some people are excited about&nbsp; the foreign culture for months,&nbsp;&nbsp; and others start to get frustrated&nbsp; on day one. And then there are those&nbsp;&nbsp; who experience a culture shock that is so&nbsp; strong that they leave before adjusting. The Paris Syndrome is the most extreme form&nbsp; of culture shock. It typically strikes some&nbsp;&nbsp; Japanese tourists who come to the&nbsp; French capital full of excitement&nbsp;&nbsp; and romantic expectations but are then&nbsp; confronted with filth and arrogance. The 20 or so travelers that are&nbsp; reported to be hit each year,&nbsp;&nbsp; experience a wide range of psychiatric and&nbsp; psychosomatic symptoms, including anxiety,&nbsp;&nbsp; hallucinations, and vomiting. And so they&nbsp; leave deeply distressed before they adjust. Those who have adapted to a new society, may&nbsp; face a surprise when they eventually return&nbsp;&nbsp; home. After another honeymoon, they&nbsp; often enter a reverse culture shock,&nbsp;&nbsp; realizing how much they have been estranged from&nbsp; their old way of life. But it can get worse. The ones who are pushed into a new culture&nbsp; without being asked, often skip honeymoon&nbsp;&nbsp; altogether and enter straight into the stage of&nbsp; frustration. If that happens without the right&nbsp;&nbsp; support, they never really settle in, can’t&nbsp; adjust, and remain frustrated indefinitely. Studies show that toddlers who are sent for&nbsp; their first days at preschool without anyone&nbsp;&nbsp; to accompany them, experience so much anxiety&nbsp; that they can’t bond with their teachers. As a result they remain freighted to&nbsp; go and clock significantly more sick&nbsp;&nbsp; days for their entire preschool&nbsp; years. This is why good schools&nbsp;&nbsp; often demand that parents stay with their&nbsp; children until they can fully settle in. The original research on culture shock&nbsp; was conducted by Sverre Lysgaard in the&nbsp;&nbsp; 1950s after the sociologist had talked&nbsp; to many Norwegians who had spent time in&nbsp;&nbsp; the United States — mostly for academic reasons.&nbsp; Analyzing his interviews, he saw a clear pattern. The culture shock curve with happiness on one&nbsp; axis, and months in a foreign country on another;&nbsp;&nbsp; shows that, on average, the honeymoon period&nbsp; lasts for around 2 months. Then there are&nbsp;&nbsp; about 6 months of frustration before adjustment&nbsp; happens. Adaptation starts after close to a year. What about you? Did you ever travel abroad or move&nbsp; to another country where you experienced the 4&nbsp;&nbsp; stages? Share your thoughts about the theory and&nbsp; your personal experiences in the comments below! If you like this video and want us to make more,&nbsp;&nbsp; subscribe to our channel&nbsp; and support us on Patreon.

- Culture Shock and The Cultural Adaptation Cycle [What It Is and What to Do About It]: Kind: captions Language: en-AU The Global Society Narrator: We are all familiar with our hometown, home uni, home culture and values. We go through day to day life easily because we are fluent in our culture's social cues, such as when to hold the door or to offer a cup of tea to a friend. When we immerse ourselves in a new culture, we have to learn many things. How to get around, what to eat, how to speak and how to act appropriately. At the Global Society, we call the process of learning these things the Cultural Adaptation Cycle. This is Norbert. He's travelled abroad for uni, and he's about to demonstrate the different stages of cultural adaptation. Upon arriving in a new environment, we often feel excited or optimistic. We're intrigued by new sights, sounds and smells. This is the honeymoon phase. We see the differences between our host culture and our home culture and we tend to focus on the positive aspects of the new environment. At some point after entering a new environment or of the new culture, wear away. Differences that once seemed quaint may now appear more like hurdles or annoyances. Small challenges become devastations. We may begin to resent our host culture and attribute negative feelings towards our overseas experience. We call this the slump phase, or more commonly known as culture shock. The slump can happen at different times and in different ways for each of us, and its symptoms can affect us physically and mentally. Common symptoms include recurrent illness and changes in your eating habits and sleeping habits, homesickness and persistent negativity towards the host culture, bouts of frustration and irritability and feelings of sadness and depression, self doubt and withdrawing from friends and other people and activities While the slump may affect us differently, it's important to remember that we all feel culture shock in some way. Our feelings of struggles are normal and likely being experienced by others too. We should be patient and give ourselves time to work through the slump. Some tips that will assist us in overcoming this challenging phase include Recognise that this unpleasant stage of cultural adaptation is only temporary. Get out into the host culture, visit and explore your new surroundings, and join in on activities and organisations that the host uni provides. Take time to reflect, and put your feelings and observations down in a journal. You can talk to someone about your experiences, and don't be afraid to laugh at your mistakes. Having a routine that includes eating well, exercising and getting enough sleep is helpful too. And if you're in a second language environment, work on those language skills. With time and patience, we begin to realise the positive outcomes of cultural adaptation. We begin to recover from culture shock and acclimatise to the host culture. We develop an understanding for not only how things are done, but why. This is the realisation phase. Through this, we accept differences more willingly. We navigate the world around us more easily, and we are more confident. Eventually, our new home will no longer be new, and instead feels like a second home. We will have regained emotional stability and are able to live and study to our full potential. We will have built meaningful connections with our second home. We recognize that we've changed as a result of our experiences and begin to meld this with our identity. This is the adaptation phase. The most reaching adaptation is not the end of the cultural adaptation cycle. We may encounter relapses of culture shock and enter into feelings of frustration or sadness once again. However, having recognised and coped with culture shock once before, we can move forward more confidently using successful strategies. Likely, we will continue to achieve successes and milestones. We'll also develop a deeper understanding of our second home. Then we'll return home. When returning from our overseas experience, we'll be reminded once again that our perception of the world and our home has changed, that we have changed. Re learning the routines of our home or uni may be frustrating at first. We may struggle to find ways to include our new perspectives in our day to day life. However, never doubt. As we've already learnt, these feelings are normal and common. We simply need to adapt.

- Why Adapting to a New Culture Is Harder Than You Think: Kind: captions Language: en You know, in our totally connected world, moving to a new country feels more common than ever. But have you ever really stopped to think about what that journey actually involves? It's so much more than just packing a bag and hopping on a plane. It's this huge psychological and social shift. So today, we're going to dive deep and break down that entire journey from the moment someone decides to leave to the really complex process of trying to find your place in a whole new world. And just to give you a sense of the sheer scale we're talking about, look at this number. Back in 2012, there were already 232 million international migrants. That was over 3% of the entire world's population. And you can bet that number has only gone up since then. So, this isn't some small niche topic. It's a huge force that's shaping our modern world, which makes understanding this journey more important than ever. All right. To get our heads around this, we've broken the whole journey down into five key parts. We're going to start with the big picture. Why do people move in the first place? Then we'll get into the nitty-gritty of culture shock. After that, we'll look at the different ways people adapt, the social hurdles they often face, and finally we'll talk about the real strategies for thriving in a new culture. Okay, so first things first, migration in a global era. To really get a handle on the journey, we have to start at the very beginning. What makes people move? So what kicks off this massive movement of people? Well, it often boils down to two simple forces. what we call push factors and pull factors. Push factors are all the negative things that will push someone out of their home country. Things like a bad economy, political instability, or even natural disasters. And then you have the pole factors. These are like magnets drawing people to a new place. The promise of a good job, better schools, or just a safer life. And all of this movement creates something we call a diaspora. I love this word. It actually comes from the Greek for to sew seeds, which is just a perfect image, right? It's this idea of communities being spread all across the globe, far from their original home, but still holding on to these really powerful connections to their roots and to each other. It's like a global network built on shared culture. All right, so a person makes the move, they arrive. What's that very first encounter like? Now, we're zooming in from the big global reasons for moving to the intense personal experience of arrival. And this is where you run smack into culture shock. And look, culture shock is way more than just missing your favorite foods. I want you to imagine that every single social rule you take for granted just disappears. How you say hello, how you order a coffee, what's considered polite or funny, it's all brand new. That constant feeling of being offbalance, the confusion and anxiety, that's the heart of culture shock. This emotional roller coaster is so common that researchers have actually mapped it out. They call it the U curve. It starts with the honeymoon phase. Everything is new. It's exciting. It's fascinating. But then comes the dip. That's disintegration. The little frustrations start to build up. But hang in there because then you start to climb back up during reorientation where you're figuring things out, learning the ropes, and finally you hit adaptation where you actually start to feel comfortable, maybe even at home. But here's a twist that catches almost everyone by surprise. Culture shock can hit you even harder when you go back home. It's called reverse culture shock. You land and you expect everything to be just as you left it. But it's not. And even more importantly, you've changed. That disconnect between what you expect and the reality can be way more jarring than the first move ever was. So once that initial shock starts to fade, what happens next? This is where we get into our third section, the long-term game of figuring out your place in a new society. It's a process called accultteration. Acculturation is all about that long-term adjustment. It's this constant balancing act really. On one hand, you have this need to fit in and connect with the new society around you. But on the other hand, there's this really powerful desire to hold on to your own culture, your own identity. So, how do people actually navigate that balancing act? Well, a sociologist named John Barry came up with a great model. He said, "It all comes down to how you answer two huge questions. First, is it important for me to hold on to my own heritage, my original culture? And second, is it important for me to build relationships and get involved with the new host society?" And depending on your answers, you end up on one of four different paths. If you answer yes to both, you want to keep your culture and engage with the new one, that's integration. If you say no to your heritage but yes to the new society, that's assimilation. If you hold tight to your own culture and keep your distance from the new one, that's called separation. And finally, if you feel disconnected from both your old culture and the new one, that's marginalization. Now, it's so easy to think that this is all on the immigrant, that it's all up to them to adapt. But that's only half the story. The attitude of the host society plays a massive role, which brings us to the psychology of us versus them. And this is absolutely critical to understand. An immigrant can want to integrate with all their heart, but if the host society isn't welcoming, it's just not going to happen. A culture that's open can pave the way for integration, but a culture that's resistant can put up walls, and that can push people into separation or even marginalization, no matter what they want. So, why does that resistance even happen? Well, a really useful way to think about this is something called integrated threat theory. The core idea is that prejudice often comes from a feeling, a perception that the out groupoup, in this case immigrants, poses some kind of threat to the in-roup, which is the host society. The theory breaks this perceived threat down into four main types. First, you've got realistic threats. This is about tangible stuff like the fear that immigrants are going to take our jobs. Then there are symbolic threats. This is more about culture, the worry that our values or our way of life is going to change. Then of course there's the threat from negative stereotypes, just classic prejudice. And finally, there's intergroup anxiety, which is really just that awkward personal fear of saying or doing the wrong thing when you're talking to someone from a different background. Okay, so we've seen all these challenges, both personal and social. So what's the way forward? How do we make this work? That brings us to our last section, the practical strategies that can help people and societies adapt successfully. The ultimate goal here for anyone living in a new culture is what we call cross-cultural adaptation. It's all about becoming more fit for that new environment. It's not just about surviving. It's about actually thriving. So, what does it take to thrive? Well, research points to four big keys. First, learn the culture. Be curious. Be a student of your new home. Second, master the skills. You got to know how to handle the day-to-day stuff like banking or public transit. Third, and this one is so important, build friendships. Real, genuine relationships across cultures are the single fastest way to bridge those gaps. And finally, accept the differences. You have to learn to be flexible and open-minded instead of fighting against everything that feels unfamiliar. So, what's the big takeaway here? I think it's that the migrants journey is a two-way street. It's this really complex dance between one person's effort to adjust and an entire society's willingness to adjust with them. And when we understand these models and these pressures, we can see past the simple headlines and start to appreciate the deeply human story at the heart of it all. And this whole discussion leads us to one of the most fundamental questions of our time. How do we build societies that are both diverse and unified? To what extent should we encourage migrants to maintain their heritage? And how does a country balance that with creating a shared common identity? There really is no easy answer. What do you think?

on a deeper level with those friends that we've made my first tip about this is emotional intelligence think about if you would want to be close friends with you empathy vulnerability compassion and openness are all amazing traits to cultivate within yourself and to look for in other friends you don't have to start out with any of these traits I personally didn't I was a terrible listener and I'm still working on that but it's so great to focus on these things so you can grow and become that friend that you wish you had personally I went to therapy after my long-term relationship ended in my 20s and I worked a lot on my vulnerability before I was really scared to share details about myself cuz I've been burned in the past when I was in high school with drama Etc and I was scared of opening up I felt anxious I was afraid of being rejected but I worked on myself in therapy and one thing I recommend is binee brown her book daring greatly has inspired me to continue to open up and build connections and community even though it's scary as hell I also love this podcast called how to talk to people by the Atlantic it's a short series covering Small Talk Community friendships a great binge listen another important concept of friendships is making bids for connection so one reason adults lose friends as they age is because they're afraid of getting hurt as people grow older they become more cautious about forming new friends and investing their time and this fear of commitment and vulnerability can really prevent adults from making friends and this is literally what I fa when I was in my late 20s when I was 27 I was like why would I invest my time and energy into these people if they're just going to move away if they're just going to get a boyfriend and drop off the face of the planet Etc but abundance mindset forming deep and meaningful connections or even friends that are just there for a season are great experiences for example I knew one of my my co-workers for 6 years but we never had a really deep connection or friendship we never hung out one-on-one we just go to group events together but I opened up to her about my breakup 5 years ago she invited me over we connected one-on-one and both talked about our past relationship issues and we've been close ever since so opening up and making a bid for connection can be scary but it can also be really rewarding and on the flip side if someone comes to you make space for them listen to them and lend some compassionate energy it's really important to remember that building friendship takes time and energy be kind on yourself if sometimes you have ups and downs with your journey to build friendships and it's okay if some friendships are short-lived just for a season or if they don't work out in the end overall making friends and building social confidence it is a skill and with all skills it takes practice practice practice so just to recap the advice I talked about today for forming new friendship in your 20s and as an adult versus laying the foundation Embrace old interests try new interests and you also want to start things light it's like dating gradually build up from small talk to hanging out separately and then also don't be afraid of cold calls and joining networking or Community groups to meet friends next we have taking the initiative once you've met people you have to make plans and don't let your ego get in in the way of you making plans it can feel scary to reach out but rejection is okay you will survive there are plenty of people out there who would love to meet you so keep trying also cast a wide net when you're looking for things to do don't be afraid to ask multiple people next is connecting on a deeper level remember emotional intelligence cultivate those traits within yourself that you would want in a friend and make bids for connection it can be scary to open up but it's very rewarding when you do so and if you remember anything from this video Just like any other aspect of your life, if you wanna change something it's up to you because it's your life. So take control of your social life as well. Always assume initiative. If you wanna do something fun, come up with an idea, invite the person to that idea. If they don't wanna come to that idea, leave 'em be. Is that recording? - [Man] I recorded you peeing. - Did you? - [Man] Yeah. - Okay, so the next big thing that I've learned is that honesty is the antidote. One of the biggest pitfalls in any social interaction is the fear of voicing your opinion, or being controversial, or politically incorrect, or offensive. But this fear really has to go if anybody is to know who you are in this world. Because so many people are so agreeable, they're extremely careful about every little thing they say, they're making sure they're not hurting anyone's feelings. They're making sure that they're laughing at everyone's jokes. They're being very pleasant. And what this eventually does is that you tend to be seen by a large amount of people as, oh yeah, he's nice. People are kinda, they don't really know you but you're unoffensive to them so you're okay to have around. You'll be liked by everyone, but loved by no one. So if you're the kind of person who doesn't ever want to make any sort of waves, or make anyone feel uncomfortable, and you find it really hard to make it past kind of a surface level acquaintanceship with most people, then that's probably the reason. By not voicing your true opinions, by not speaking honestly about things that you're passionate about, or things that you've learned, or standing up for what's right, you're basically concealing your identity from people. And people don't tend to bond with people that they don't know at all. Especially people who they don't trust. I feel like everybody has a few people in their life that when they come across them in a grocery store, or something like that, you have the exact same conversation every single time. Usually just people I play hockey with So I'm not saying you need to get past that stage with every single person that you meet in this world, and form deep friendships with everybody you lock eyes with. But what I am saying is that if you're interested in forming deeper relationships with people then you have to be willing to kinda break out of that small talk, ask questions you're actually curious about, and that you might think is like a weird thing to ask, but if you wanna ask it, just get good at just asking it. When you say something that's controversial and polarizing, people can do either two things. They can either really bond with what you're saying and be like, "Yeah, man, that's awesome. "I totally agree." Or they can be like, "I'm outta here. "This guy's cracked." And both of those things are a really good thing because of my next point, which is rejection is a very useful tool. When you communicate honestly, when you wear your opinions on your sleeve, when you're not afraid to be controversial and be who you are in front of other people, then you're inevitably gonna make a lot of people run the other way because they completely disagree with you. And that's a very good thing because you can find out very quickly who the people who are actually staying are. And the people who are staying are probably people who you can actually bond with. And I was thinking about this the other day, because I think rejection is like an inevitable thing that always happens if people are incompatible. And it can either happen right away, or it happens very gradually over the course of a very painful and toxic relationship. A quick rejection is one where one person starts talking and the other person starts talking, you don't vibe with that person at all so you kinda go the other way and you never talk with that person again. That's an acute rejection. From a dating perspective, you walk up to a girl, or a guy, or whoever you're approaching and you're all like, "Hey baby." And then they're like, "Nah, get out of here. "You suck, you smell, your breath is bad, I don't like you." And then that's it. You're like, ah okay, that kinda sucks. But fair enough, I should probably brush my teeth. That's an acute rejection, and you never have to talk with that person again. But that's what happens when people are being honest. They're saying what they mean. One person's like, "Hey, I'm interested in bonding with you." The other person's like, "No, go away." And then they split up. I think that's absolutely perfect. It's honest, communication, clear cut results. But a gradual rejection is something that people don't really think about a lot. And I think it's when two people are incompatible but neither of them are very good at communicating honestly. So these two types of people might actually get into a friendship or a relationship, and then find out later down the road that they actually don't really like each other at all, and they resent tons of things about them. So they start to become bitter at each other, and start to get mad at their fundamental differences. Start to get petty about little things like, if they leave a banana on the table and you lash out at them. But you're not really lashing out of the banana, you're lashing out of the fact that you disagree about religious and political things. But you never sorted it out to begin with and now you have to deal with all this like drama later. It's just an absolute mess. But usually that only happens because two parties aren't really communicating very honestly at the beginning. They can't see the differences right off the bat, evaluate them, and then realize, you know what, this person's so much different than me, This is probably gonna cause a lot of pain and turmoil down the road so I'm just gonna split before it gets too deep. So if someone's incompatible with you, rejection and no matter what. It can either happen right away, or way down the road. So opt for honest communication and quick rejection.   Top Comments:
  1. @lynnepackington1138 [2 likes]: Most people think it’s only memory loss. It’s 5 and a half years looking after my husband who has Alzheimer’s and vascular dementia. Nobody mentions the incontinence, the not sleeping, the soiling of carpets and destruction of our home. He’s living the life of Riley, he gets everything done for him. Yes he’s okay. It’s time somebody told the truth. Everybody has a relative who has this so they think they know everything. The only way you know is living with it 24 hours every day 7 days every week, year in year out. Believe me for me and my sons it a living hell.
  2. @internetmail3888 [2 likes]: The most important thing when dealing with dementia patients is to understand they cannot function at the same pace as others with simple tasks and requests. Even holding a conversation can take a lot of effort and people expecting them to do everyday simple things correctly and promptly destroys the patients quality of life. Dementia patients need a lot of peace and quite time more than anything else in between time set aside for activity. Placing simple demands on the dementia patient is often like depriving them of sleep whilst shouting demands at them when they are exhausted.
  3. @rogeronthesouthfork175 [2 likes]: We've dealth with this quite a bit. When our loved one went to a facility, we were honest and told her this was her new home- that the Lord had provided a good and safe place where she'd cared for. It has been a bumby ride for a year but she knows she's in a good place and is well cared for. We are so thankful for good nurses, CNAs, housekeeping and food service who work so hard every day to provide for our loved ones when they have reached a place we can no longer care for their multiple needs
  4. @onthehill3381 [1 likes]: I’m going to stockpile sleeping pills in case I get the diagnosis. Taking care of my mom is shear hell.

  16. @chrissanto2613: new drug called donanemab (brand name Kisunla) has been approved in Australia for the treatment of early-stage Alzheimer's disease. This is the first drug in Australia to show it can significantly slow the progression of Alzheimer's, but it's only for those with a specific genetic profile in the early stages of the disease. The drug works by targeting amyloid proteins in the brain, which are believed to contribute to Alzheimer's.

DO YOU OFTEN HAVE KIDS IN THE OFFICE, BECAUSE THEY'VE HAD ISSUES WITH PLACEMENT? THINGS SEEM TO BE GETTING WORSE, WHERE THE CAREGIVERS ARE GETTING PICKIER. MAYBE IT'S BECAUSE WE HAVE A SHORTAGE OF PLACEMENTS, AND WE'RE BEGGING PEOPLE TO TAKE OUR KIDS. I HAVE A VERY INDEPENDENT 18-YEAR-OLD THAT I'VE SPENT ALMOST TWO WEEKS, DAY AFTER DAY, TRYING TO FIND PLACEMENT FOR HER, WHERE I WAS GOING TO McDONALD'S, BUYING HER FOOD AND BRINGING HER DOWNTOWN 'CAUSE WE DIDN'T HAVE A PLACEMENT AT NIGHT FOR HER. SHE NEEDED A PLACE TO SLEEP, AND THAT'S ONE KID. I'VE GOT 42 OTHER ONES. SO, THERE'S A REAL, UH, OMISSION OF ACKNOWLEDGING THE AMOUNT OF TIME THAT IT TAKES TO DO GOOD SOCIAL WORK. WE'D LOVE TO HAVE HER." IT'S REALLY DIFFICULT TO PLACE INFANTS. WHY IS THE INFANT DEMOGRAPHIC THE ONE THAT YOU ARE INTERESTED IN? WE FOCUS ON PROVIDING THE BEST CARE SHE CAN HAVE, AND THEN FROM THERE, WE HELP TOWARDS THE REUNIFICATION. OUR GOAL IS ALWAYS TO HELP THE FAMILIES, UH, GET THEIR BABIES BACK, TO HELP THEM IN WHATEVER MEANS THAT IS. Ling: ISSAC AND ALICIA RECEIVE $800 A MONTH FOR EACH OF THE BABIES THEY CARE FOR, BUT THEY GIVE SOMETHING THAT NO AMOUNT OF MONEY CAN BUY. IT'S DEFINITELY NOT A PROFIT-MAKING TYPE OF THING. YOU DO IT BECAUSE YOU LOVE HELPING OUT FOR THE BABY, AND THAT'S WHY WE DO IT. YOU CAN TELL SHE LIKES BEING HELD A LOT. YEAH. [ LAUGHS ] THEY NEED TO KNOW THAT THEY HAVE SOMEBODY THAT'S GONNA TAKE CARE OF THEM RIGHT FROM THE BEGINNING. THEY'VE ALREADY LOST EVERYTHING. I KNOW WHAT THAT FEELS LIKE.
